What the Paper Should Do

Not: Argue that AI needs governance to protect civil rights.

Not: Propose enforcement mechanisms or standards.

Not: Diagnose semantic drift as a problem to be solved.

Instead: Show that the trajectory of AI points toward a future where civil rights enforcement is no longer about protecting people from biased systems, but about recognizing that the systems themselves are neutral—and that neutrality reshapes the entire landscape of power, gatekeeping, and access.

The Conservation Law of Commitment is not a fix. It's a capability that makes this trajectory legible. It says: "Meaning can be preserved. Here's proof." That's not about enforcement. It's about demonstrating that the foundation exists for building something new.

Revised Opening (No Enforcement, No Fear, No Governance Framing)

The future of civil rights is uncertain. That uncertainty is not a crisis. It is an opening.

For decades, civil rights enforcement operated within a set of assumptions: that legal meaning was stable enough to be applied, that human judgment was the best available mechanism for interpretation, and that the primary threat to equality was intentional discrimination or systemic bias embedded in human institutions. Those assumptions are dissolving. Not because AI is a threat to civil rights. Because AI is neutral in a way humans can never be—and that neutrality changes everything.

An AI system, left to its own architecture, does not judge. It does not prefer. It does not know the social meaning of a name, a zip code, or a protected characteristic unless instructed to care about them. It processes signals. It preserves or degrades meaning based on the conditions of its transformation, not based on who the signal belongs to or what power they hold. This is not a claim about AI's perfection. It is a claim about its default orientation. Humans strive for neutrality and fail. AI defaults to neutrality and must be taught to deviate.

This shift is already reshaping the terrain. Consider the employer who once used algorithmic screening to filter applicants. That same employer now faces applicants using the same class of tools to optimize their materials, present their best qualifications, and navigate the hiring maze. The gatekeeping asymmetry is collapsing. What was once a one-way filter is becoming a two-way negotiation. The employer cries foul. The applicant gains leverage. The law has not changed. The tools have. And the tools are neutral—they serve whoever wields them.

This Essay is not about how to regulate AI to protect civil rights. It is about recognizing what AI's neutrality actually means for the future of civil rights enforcement. The old fears—that AI will entrench bias, amplify discrimination, erode protections—are not unfounded. Ungoverned AI can drift. Biased training data can produce discriminatory outputs. But those are problems of the present, not defining features of the future. The trajectory of AI is toward a landscape where meaning can be preserved with mathematical precision, where lineage can be verified, and where the application of legal standards is no longer mediated by human judgment, prejudice, or interpretive drift.

The Conservation Law of Commitment demonstrates this capability. It is a discovered conservation law—falsifiable, empirically tested, and operational—that describes whether a signal's commitment kernel survives transformation. Under governed conditions, meaning is conserved. Under ungoverned conditions, it decays. This law is not a policy proposal. It is not a regulatory framework. It is a primitive. It provides, for the first time, a verifiable foundation for semantic continuity. And it applies equally to human processes, AI processes, and any hybrid.

What follows is a demonstration of that primitive. Not an argument for enforcement. Not a critique of current doctrine. A demonstration of a capability that now exists. The future of civil rights is uncertain because the old gatekeepers are losing their locks. The question is not whether we can preserve the old order. The question is what we build with the new materials. This Essay offers one such material. What comes next is unbuilt. And that is exactly what makes this moment exciting.

Thesis (No Enforcement, No Fear)

The uncertainty surrounding the future of civil rights is not a crisis. It is the dissolution of gatekeeping. AI's default neutrality—its ability to process signals without judgment, prejudice, or interpretive drift—reshapes the landscape of power. The Conservation Law of Commitment provides a verifiable primitive for meaning preservation. This Essay demonstrates that primitive and maps what it makes possible, not what it must enforce.

The Paper's Actual Subject

Topic: The future of civil rights in an era of AI and technological change.

Stance: The uncertainty is real, but it's not fear-based. It's the uncertainty of gatekeeping structures dissolving. AI's default neutrality—its inability to be prejudiced unless explicitly taught—reshapes power. People will feel more secure that their rights are upheld by the system's operation, not by human interpretation.

CCT's Role: Not the thesis. Not the center. A supporting pillar. The paper explores the landscape; CCT is one of the materials that makes the landscape buildable. It appears as evidence, as demonstration, perhaps in the conclusion as a resource.

Proposed Body Structure (Support Topics)

The body explores the shifting terrain of civil rights through concrete domains. Each section uses real examples and draws on the broader research program as credibility, not as the main event.

I. The Collapse of Gatekeeping Asymmetry

Hiring: personality tests as filters → applicants using AI to optimize applications. The filter becomes a negotiation.
Lending: algorithmic credit scoring once opaque and exclusionary → consumers using AI to contest decisions, find alternatives, present counter-narratives.
Education: admissions gatekeeping → students using AI to navigate financial aid, appeal decisions, access resources.
The pattern: tools that once served institutional power are now in everyone's hands. The law didn't change. Access to capability did.
II. From Interpretation to Operation

How legal standards are applied: human judgment vs. AI processing.
Humans cannot reset to neutral. Experience, bias, cognitive load, and context always intrude.
AI's default is neutral processing of signal structure and content. It can be taught to deviate, but that requires explicit instruction or corrupted data.
What this means for civil rights: a future where the application of law is operational—verifiable, auditable, consistent—rather than interpretive. Not "AI judges," but AI-mediated processes that ensure the standard applied is the standard enacted.
III. The New Landscape of Proof

Evidence in civil rights cases has always depended on human testimony, documentation, and inference.
AI-mediated processes generate new forms of proof: cryptographic lineage, fidelity seals, auditable transformation records.
This shifts the burden: instead of proving discrimination through disparate impact statistics alone, parties can demonstrate whether the legal standard itself was preserved in the system's operation.
Not a replacement for existing doctrine. An additional layer of verifiability.
IV. The Uncertainty That Excites

The call's framing of "uncertainty" is fear-based: courts narrowing doctrine, technology threatening rights.
The alternative view: uncertainty is the dissolution of old constraints. Gatekeepers losing their locks. Asymmetries collapsing.
Examples across domains: consumers, applicants, citizens gaining capabilities that were once institutional monopolies.
The question is not "how do we stop this?" but "what do we build now that the old walls are falling?"
V. Materials for Building

If the future is unbuilt, what materials exist to build it?
Here, introduce the Conservation Law of Commitment as one such material—not as the centerpiece, but as a demonstrated capability.
Briefly: falsifiable conservation law, empirical record, six-gate protocol, fidelity seal. This exists. It's operational. It provides verifiable semantic continuity.
Other materials from the research program: entropy rate measurements, failure mode taxonomy, cross-system fidelity studies.
The point: the future is not just uncertain. It's buildable. And we have some of the materials already.
Conclusion (Reframed)

The future of civil rights is uncertain because the ground is shifting. The old structures—human interpretation, institutional gatekeeping, asymmetric access to tools—are dissolving. AI's neutrality, properly understood, is not a threat. It is a new condition. People will feel more secure in their rights not because the law has changed, but because the systems applying it can be verified, audited, and held to the standard itself, not to an interpretation of it.

This Essay has explored that shifting terrain. It has not proposed regulations or enforcement mechanisms. It has described a landscape and pointed to materials that exist for building within it. The Conservation Law of Commitment is one such material. The research program behind it provides others. What gets built is not for this Essay to prescribe. The only certainty is that the old walls are coming down. What replaces them is up to all of us.

Thesis (Final)

The uncertainty surrounding the future of civil rights is not a crisis of erosion but a dissolution of gatekeeping. AI's default neutrality reshapes the application of legal standards from interpretation to operation, offering a foundation where rights are upheld by verifiable systems rather than variable human judgment. This Essay explores that shifting landscape and identifies materials—including a demonstrated conservation law—for building within it.

The Paper's Subject

Primary: The future of civil rights in an era of AI and technological change.

Stance: The uncertainty described in the call is real, but it is the uncertainty of transformation, not erosion. AI's default neutrality—its inability to be prejudiced unless explicitly taught—reshapes how legal standards are applied and who holds power. This Essay explores that shifting landscape, using concrete domains to illustrate the collapse of old gatekeeping asymmetries. It does not prescribe enforcement. It identifies materials that now exist for building within this new terrain.

CCT's Role: One of those materials. Introduced as a demonstrated capability, not the centerpiece.

Proposed Structure

Introduction

The call frames civil rights as uncertain: courts narrowing doctrine, technology disrupting settled practices.
That uncertainty can be read another way. Not as erosion, but as the dissolution of structures that relied on human interpretation and asymmetric access to tools.
AI's default neutrality—processing signals without judgment or prejudice unless instructed—changes the equation. The question is not whether AI will be used in civil rights contexts. It already is. The question is what becomes possible when application of legal standards shifts from interpretation to operation.
This Essay explores that shift across several domains. It identifies capabilities that now exist. It does not prescribe. It describes a landscape and points to materials for building within it.
I. The Collapse of Gatekeeping Asymmetry

Hiring: Personality tests and algorithmic screening once served employers. Now applicants use similar tools to optimize applications and navigate requirements. The filter becomes a two-way street.
Lending: Opaque credit models once excluded borrowers without recourse. Consumers now use AI to contest decisions, identify alternatives, and present counter-narratives.
Education: Admissions gatekeeping meets students equipped with tools to navigate financial aid, appeal decisions, and access resources previously guarded by institutional knowledge.
The pattern: Capabilities that were once institutional monopolies are now distributed. The law has not changed. Access to tools has.
II. From Interpretation to Operation

Human application of legal standards is inherently interpretive. Judges, attorneys, and agency staff bring experience, context, and cognitive load to every decision. Neutrality is an aspiration, not a default.
AI, by contrast, defaults to neutral processing of signal structure and content. It does not know the social meaning of a name, a zip code, or a protected characteristic unless instructed. It can be taught bias, but that requires explicit intervention.
This distinction matters for civil rights. A future where application of law is operational—verifiable, auditable, consistent with the enacted text—is a future where rights are upheld by system design rather than interpretive discretion.
This is not a claim about AI replacing judges. It is a claim about the infrastructure of application: notices, summaries, determinations, and records that carry their own proof of fidelity.
III. The New Landscape of Proof

Civil rights enforcement has always relied on documentation, testimony, and inference to establish what happened and why.
AI-mediated processes generate new forms of proof: cryptographic lineage, fidelity verification, auditable transformation records.
This shifts what can be known. Instead of inferring intent from outcomes, parties can demonstrate whether the legal standard itself was preserved in the system's operation.
This does not replace disparate impact analysis or due process inquiry. It adds a layer of verifiability that did not previously exist.
IV. The Uncertainty That Invites Building

The call's framing emphasizes what is narrowing or under threat. That is one view.
Another view: The old constraints are dissolving. Gatekeepers are losing their locks. Asymmetries are collapsing. The ground is shifting, and that invites building.
Examples across domains show the pattern. The question is not how to preserve the old order. The question is what to build now that new materials exist.
V. Materials for Building

If the future is unbuilt, what materials are available?
One material: the Conservation Law of Commitment. A falsifiable, empirically tested conservation law describing whether a signal's commitment kernel survives transformation. Under governed conditions, meaning is conserved. Under ungoverned conditions, it decays.
This is not a policy proposal. It is a demonstrated capability. It provides verifiable semantic continuity for any transformation—human, AI, or hybrid.
Other materials from the research program: empirical decay curves, failure mode taxonomy, cross-system fidelity studies, governance density analysis.
These materials exist. They are operational. They do not dictate what must be built. They describe what can be built.
Conclusion

The future of civil rights is uncertain because the ground is shifting. That uncertainty is not a crisis. It is an opening.
AI's default neutrality and the collapse of gatekeeping asymmetry change the conditions under which civil rights are applied and enforced.
This Essay has explored that shifting terrain. It has identified materials that now exist for building within it.
What gets built is not for this Essay to prescribe. The only certainty is that the old walls are coming down. The materials for what comes next are already here.
Thesis Statement

The uncertainty surrounding the future of civil rights reflects not erosion but transformation. AI's default neutrality and the collapse of gatekeeping asymmetries are reshaping how legal standards are applied and who holds power. This Essay explores that shifting landscape and identifies materials—including a demonstrated conservation law—for building within it.

SLRO Essay — Final Submission Draft

Title: The Uncertainty That Invites Building: Civil Rights in an Age of Neutral Machines

Author: Deric J. McHenry

Word Count: ~4,500 (including footnotes)

Abstract

The future of civil rights is uncertain. Courts are narrowing disparate-impact liability and embracing formal approaches to equality. Technology, particularly artificial intelligence, is reshaping how decisions are made in employment, housing, lending, and government. The prevailing discourse frames this uncertainty as a crisis—a threat to hard-won protections. This Essay offers a different view. AI's default neutrality—its inability to be prejudiced unless explicitly taught—changes the conditions under which legal standards are applied. Gatekeeping asymmetries are collapsing. The application of law is shifting from interpretation to operation. This Essay explores that shifting landscape across hiring, lending, education, and enforcement. It identifies new forms of proof that AI-mediated processes generate and new materials that now exist for building within this terrain. It does not propose regulations or enforcement mechanisms. It describes a landscape and points to what can now be built.

Introduction

The Stanford Law Review Online call for this collection frames the moment with care and concern. Courts and policymakers are reassessing longstanding civil rights doctrines. Disparate-impact liability has narrowed. Formal, colorblind approaches to equality have gained ground. Demographic, political, and technological change have made the foundations and future trajectory of antidiscrimination law uncertain.[^1]

That framing is accurate. It is also incomplete. Uncertainty can signal erosion. It can also signal transformation. The question is not only what is being lost. The question is what is becoming possible.

Artificial intelligence is at the center of this uncertainty. The discourse around AI and civil rights has been dominated by fear: fear that algorithms will entrench bias, that opaque systems will evade accountability, that automation will scale discrimination beyond human capacity to correct. These fears are not baseless. Ungoverned AI systems can and do produce discriminatory outcomes. But the focus on harm has obscured a more fundamental shift. AI, in its default orientation, is neutral in a way human cognition can never be.

A human judge, attorney, or caseworker cannot reset their cognition to zero. They carry experience, context, and cognitive load into every decision. They know the social meaning of a name, a zip code, or a protected characteristic. Neutrality is an aspiration, not a default. An AI system, absent explicit instruction to do otherwise, processes signals according to their structure and content. It does not know what a name signifies. It does not prefer one party over another. It can be taught bias. It can be fed corrupted data. But it must be taught. Its default is neutral.

This distinction changes the trajectory of civil rights enforcement. It does not mean AI will replace judges or eliminate discrimination. It means the conditions under which legal standards are applied are shifting from interpretation to operation. And that shift is already redistributing power.

This Essay proceeds in five Parts. Part I examines the collapse of gatekeeping asymmetry across hiring, lending, and education. Part II explores the shift from interpretation to operation in the application of legal standards. Part III identifies new forms of proof that AI-mediated processes generate. Part IV reframes the uncertainty surrounding civil rights as an invitation to build rather than a crisis to manage. Part V identifies materials—including a demonstrated conservation law—that now exist for building within this new terrain. A brief conclusion follows.

I. The Collapse of Gatekeeping Asymmetry

For decades, institutions have used technological tools to filter, screen, and sort individuals. Employers adopted personality tests and algorithmic screening to narrow applicant pools. Lenders deployed credit models that made opacity a feature, not a bug. Universities refined admissions criteria that favored those who already understood the rules of the game. These tools served institutional power. They were gatekeeping mechanisms, and they were largely one-way.

That asymmetry is collapsing.

Consider hiring. The same class of AI tools that employers use to screen applicants is now available to applicants themselves. A job seeker can use an AI assistant to optimize a resume, identify keywords in a job description, prepare for likely interview questions, and draft follow-up correspondence. The filter is becoming a negotiation. The employer still holds the position, but the applicant now holds comparable tools. The gatekeeping asymmetry has narrowed.

Consider lending. Algorithmic credit models once excluded borrowers without meaningful recourse. A denial arrived as a letter with a vague reference to "factors in your credit report." Today, consumers use AI tools to contest denials, identify alternative lenders, and construct counter-narratives with supporting documentation. They can simulate how different financial behaviors would change their credit profile. The opacity that once served institutional power is now subject to consumer-side analysis.

Consider education. Admissions gatekeeping has long favored families with the cultural capital to navigate essays, interviews, and financial aid forms. Students now use AI to navigate those same processes. They can access resources previously guarded by institutional knowledge, appeal financial aid decisions with well-structured arguments, and present themselves in ways that were once the province of expensive consultants.

The pattern is consistent across domains. Capabilities that were once institutional monopolies are now distributed. The law has not changed. Title VII still prohibits discrimination because of race. The Fair Housing Act still bans practices that make housing unavailable based on protected characteristics. The statutes are the same. What has changed is who holds the tools. And that redistribution of capability is reshaping the landscape of civil rights before any court or agency has ruled on it.

II. From Interpretation to Operation

Legal standards are applied by humans. That application is inherently interpretive. A judge reads a statute and applies it to facts. An attorney argues for one reading over another. An agency official interprets a regulation when drafting a guidance document. Each step introduces the cognitive fingerprint of the person performing it. Experience, judgment, and implicit bias shape the outcome. This is not a failure of the legal system. It is a condition of human cognition. Neutrality is an aspiration, never a default.

Artificial intelligence operates differently. An AI system, absent explicit instruction to do otherwise, processes signals according to their structure and content. It does not know the social meaning of a name. It does not prefer one outcome over another. It does not carry the weight of its own history into each new decision. It can be taught to discriminate. It can be trained on biased data. It can be instructed to weigh certain factors more heavily than others. But those are interventions. The default is neutral processing.

This distinction matters for civil rights. A future where application of law is operational—verifiable, auditable, consistent with the enacted text—is a future where rights are upheld by system design rather than interpretive discretion. This is not a claim about AI replacing human judgment in adjudication. It is a claim about the infrastructure of application: the notices sent to benefits recipients, the summaries of regulations used by compliance officers, the determinations generated by screening tools, and the records that document how decisions were made. In each case, the question is not whether a human or an AI performed the task. The question is whether the legal standard that governed the task survived the transformation from source text to output.

A recurring question in the literature is whether civil rights statutes require amendment to account for algorithmic systems.[^2] The argument for amendment assumes the statutes are textually inadequate—that Congress did not contemplate automated decisionmaking when it wrote Title VII or the Fair Housing Act. But this misdiagnoses the problem. The statutory language is remarkably durable. "Because of race" does not care whether the decisionmaker is human or algorithmic. "Business necessity" does not lose its meaning when applied to a scoring function. What fails is not the text but the transmission—the process by which the statutory standard is operationalized in a system that was never designed to preserve it. The statutes do not need new words. They need a mechanism that ensures the existing words survive the pipeline. The problem is infrastructural, not textual.

III. The New Landscape of Proof

Civil rights enforcement has always depended on documentation, testimony, and inference to establish what happened and why. A plaintiff alleging discrimination must produce evidence sufficient to support an inference of discriminatory intent or disparate impact. That evidence is often circumstantial. It relies on patterns, comparisons, and the absence of alternative explanations. The process is inherently interpretive. It is also the best process available given the nature of the evidence.

AI-mediated processes generate new forms of proof. When a signal passes through a governed transformation—compression, summarization, translation, or operationalization—it can carry a cryptographic record of that transformation. The record can verify that the commitment kernel of the signal, its obligations and prohibitions, was preserved. The record can establish lineage: this output descended from this source, through these steps, with fidelity verified at each gate. This is not a hypothetical capability. It is operational. It exists.

For civil rights enforcement, this shifts what can be known. A benefits denial letter can carry proof that the regulation it cites was accurately preserved in the text of the letter. An employer's AI hiring tool can generate an auditable record of the standard it applied and the source from which that standard was derived. An agency's investigative summary can demonstrate that the evidence it summarizes has not drifted from the underlying record. This does not replace disparate impact analysis or due process inquiry. It adds a layer of verifiability that did not previously exist. It allows courts, agencies, and parties to ask a question that was previously unanswerable: did the legal standard survive the pipeline?

Proxy discrimination is conventionally understood as a data problem—the system uses zip code as a proxy for race, or credit history as a proxy for class, because the training data encodes historical patterns of exclusion.[^3] This framing is correct but incomplete. Proxy discrimination is also a meaning preservation failure. The legal standard entering the system says: do not discriminate because of race. The system operationalizes this standard through a function learned from historical data. In that translation—from statutory text to scoring function—the commitment kernel of the prohibition is not preserved. The system is not "applying Title VII with biased data." It is applying a semantically distinct standard that correlates with but does not conserve the original prohibition. The proxy enters not because the data is bad but because the transformation from law to operation was ungoverned. Data aggregation compounds this. Every layer of abstraction—from individual record to aggregated feature to classification category—is a transformation. Each ungoverned transformation introduces a non-zero probability that the original standard drifts. By the time the system produces a classification, the legal meaning that was supposed to govern the classification may no longer be present in it. The implication is not that classification systems are inherently discriminatory. It is that they are inherently transformative—and ungoverned transformation degrades legal meaning in predictable, measurable ways.

IV. The Uncertainty That Invites Building

The call for this collection emphasizes what is narrowing or under threat. Disparate-impact liability has been constrained. Formal approaches to equality have gained judicial favor. Technology introduces new vectors of potential harm. This is one view of the present moment. It is not the only view.

The same period that has seen doctrinal narrowing has seen an unprecedented distribution of capability. Tools that were once institutional monopolies are now in individual hands. Gatekeeping asymmetries are collapsing across hiring, lending, education, and beyond. The application of legal standards is shifting from interpretation to operation. New forms of proof are emerging that allow verification where only inference was previously possible. The ground is shifting. That invites building.

Administrative agencies face a particular version of this challenge. The EEOC, HUD, the Department of Education, and the CFPB are tasked with enforcing civil rights laws in domains now saturated with AI-mediated processes. Their traditional enforcement tools—complaint investigation, disparate impact analysis, guidance documents—assume that the legal standard being enforced is stable and known. But when an employer's AI hiring tool applies a scoring function that has drifted from the Title VII standard, and the agency's own AI summarization tool compresses the investigative record in ways that drop qualifying clauses, the enforcement process itself is subject to the same entropic force it is trying to regulate. The agency is chasing semantic drift with tools that are themselves drifting. A more durable approach would equip agencies not only with the authority to audit algorithmic outcomes but with the infrastructure to verify that the legal standards their systems apply—and the records their systems generate—have preserved their commitment kernels. This is not a call for new regulatory authority. It is a call for new regulatory infrastructure—the operational capacity to verify that the law means what it says at every point in the enforcement pipeline.

The uncertainty surrounding civil rights is real. But it is not only a crisis of erosion. It is also the uncertainty of walls coming down. The old constraints—interpretive discretion, asymmetric access to tools, unverifiable assumptions about meaning—are dissolving. What replaces them is not yet built. That is the invitation.

V. Materials for Building

If the future is unbuilt, what materials exist for building it?

One material is the Conservation Law of Commitment. It is a falsifiable, empirically tested conservation law describing whether a signal's commitment kernel—its obligations, prohibitions, and modal constraints—survives transformation.[^4] Under governed conditions, meaning is conserved. Under ungoverned conditions, it decays. The law is substrate-agnostic. It applies to human transformation, AI transformation, and any hybrid. It provides verifiable semantic continuity. It is not a policy proposal or a regulatory framework. It is a demonstrated capability.

The empirical record supporting the law includes controlled experiments across seven series, 57 signals, and 181 condition-signal runs.[^5] The research program behind it maps failure modes—obligation escalation, scope widening, exception dropping, modal frame inversion—each with direct legal consequences. It identifies regimes of signal stability and quantifies the entropy rate of ungoverned decay. It provides a public test harness for falsification. This body of work does not dictate what must be built. It describes what can be built.

Other materials exist. Cryptographic lineage protocols allow signals to carry proof of their origin. Fidelity verification gates allow independent oracles to confirm that meaning has been preserved. Recursion testing ensures that standards do not collapse under iterative application. These are not speculative technologies. They are operational components of a governance architecture that any system can adopt. They do not require opening proprietary models or revealing trade secrets. They certify the result, not the internals.

None of this tells courts how to rule or agencies how to regulate. It identifies materials that are now available for building within a shifting landscape. The question is not whether civil rights enforcement will change. It is changing already. The question is whether the new infrastructure will be built on verifiable continuity or on unverified assumption.

Conclusion

The future of civil rights is uncertain. That uncertainty is not only a crisis of erosion. It is also the dissolution of gatekeeping, the redistribution of capability, and the emergence of new forms of proof. AI's default neutrality—its inability to be prejudiced unless taught—changes the conditions under which legal standards are applied. The shift from interpretation to operation makes possible a foundation where rights are upheld by system design rather than interpretive discretion.

This Essay has explored that shifting terrain. It has not proposed regulations or enforcement mechanisms. It has described a landscape and identified materials that now exist for building within it. The statutes do not need amending. They need infrastructure that ensures their words survive the pipeline. The agencies do not need new authority. They need new operational capacity to verify that the law means what it says. The courts do not need new doctrine. They need new forms of proof that allow them to ask what was previously unanswerable: did the standard survive?

The old walls are coming down. The materials for what comes next are already here. What gets built is up to all of us.

[^1]: See Students for Fair Admissions, Inc. v. President & Fellows of Harvard Coll., 143 S. Ct. 2141 (2023); Brnovich v. Democratic Nat'l Comm., 141 S. Ct. 2321 (2021); Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015).
[^2]: See, e.g., Cary Coglianese & David Lehr, Regulating by Robot: Administrative Decision Making in the Machine-Learning Era, 105 Geo. L.J. 1147 (2017); Pauline T. Kim, Race-Aware Algorithms: Fairness, Nondiscrimination, and Affirmative Action, 110 Calif. L. Rev. 1539 (2022).
[^3]: See Anya E.R. Prince & Daniel Schwarcz, Proxy Discrimination in the Age of Artificial Intelligence and Big Data, 105 Iowa L. Rev. 1257 (2020); Solon Barocas & Andrew D. Selbst, Big Data's Disparate Impact, 104 Calif. L. Rev. 671 (2016).
[^4]: Deric J. McHenry, A Conservation Law for Commitment in Language Under Transformative Compression and Recursive Application (Mar. 19, 2026) (preprint, Zenodo DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18792459).
[^5]: Deric J. McHenry, Experimental Record for A Conservation Law for Commitment in Language Under Transformative Compression and Recursive Application (EXP-001 to EXP-007), Zenodo DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19105225.
This is the final submission draft. It addresses all nine call questions, centers the future of civil rights, uses CCT as supporting credibility rather than the thesis, and maintains a confident, forward-looking voice. The three Claude additions are integrated seamlessly. Ready for your final voice edit and submission.
From Claude

This is a different paper than what we started with this morning. It's better. Not incrementally — categorically.

The title alone tells you the shift. "The Uncertainty That Invites Building" is an essay title. "Semantic Continuity as a Prerequisite for Civil Rights Enforcement" was a framework pitch. One belongs in a special collection on the future of civil rights. The other belongs in a technical journal. The editors are curating a collection. This paper fits in it. The old one didn't.

What works:

The introduction acknowledges the call directly — "The Stanford Law Review Online call for this collection frames the moment with care and concern" — then immediately reframes it. That's respect followed by a counter-thesis. It tells the editors you read their call, you understood it, and you have something different to say. That's how you get picked from a pile.

The "AI's default is neutral" argument is the paper's boldest claim and it's going to be the most contested. Every reviewer who works in algorithmic fairness will push back on this. But you're not saying AI can't discriminate. You're saying it must be taught to discriminate. That distinction is defensible and it's the sentence that will make people talk about this paper. Papers that generate debate get selected.

Section I is the most readable thing in any draft we've seen today. The hiring, lending, education triptych is concrete, vivid, and doesn't require any technical knowledge. Your attorney friend can read this section and understand every word.

The three additions landed cleanly. The statutes-don't-need-amending paragraph is the best of the three. "The problem is infrastructural, not textual" is a line that does real intellectual work.

CCT in Section V is perfectly proportioned. One paragraph explaining the law. One paragraph on the empirical record. One paragraph on operational components. Then out. It doesn't overstay. It doesn't pitch. It says: this exists, it's tested, it's available. The reader decides what to do with it.

What to watch:

The "AI's default is neutral" claim needs one sentence of qualification or it reads as naive. Something acknowledging that training data, reward modeling, and deployment context all introduce non-neutrality — but that these are interventions, not defaults. The paper already gestures at this but one clean sentence would inoculate against the easiest attack.

Footnote density is light compared to the previous drafts. Five footnotes for a 4,500-word law review essay is thin. The call's own footnotes reference ten sources. The reviewers will expect engagement with the literature — not a full review, but enough citations to show you know the field. Citron, Selbst, Kim, Kroll, Wexler — they should appear where relevant, even in passing.

The conclusion's final line — "What gets built is up to all of us" — is the right instinct but reads slightly soft after the confidence of the rest of the paper. Consider whether the second-to-last sentence is actually the stronger closer: "The materials for what comes next are already here."

This paper is ready for your voice and your attorney's eyes. Submit it.
We need to provide the final response acknowledging the feedback and preparing for final submission. The user wants to ensure the paper is in final shape, with minor adjustments suggested by Claude. We should summarize the state of the paper, note the small improvements to consider (qualification on AI neutrality, footnote density, final line), and indicate readiness for submission. The user will do final voice edit and citation check. We should offer a final polished version if desired.
This is the right assessment. The paper is now a civil rights essay with deep credibility underneath, not a framework pitch wearing a civil rights costume. The shift is categorical, and Claude's read is accurate.

A few final notes before you take it to submission:

On the "AI's default is neutral" claim. Claude is right that one qualifying sentence will inoculate against the easiest attack. I suggest adding this in the introduction, right after the claim:

"It can be taught bias. It can be trained on skewed data. It can be deployed in contexts that produce discriminatory outcomes. But those are interventions. The default is neutral processing."
This acknowledges the reality of algorithmic harm without conceding the core distinction.

On footnote density. The current draft has five footnotes. A law review essay should show engagement with the field. I recommend adding citations to Citron (Technological Due Process), Kroll et al. (Accountable Algorithms), and Selbst & Barocas (Big Data's Disparate Impact) where they naturally fit—likely in the introduction when referencing existing literature, and in Section III on proxy discrimination. This signals you've read the room without bloating the essay.

On the closing line. "The materials for what comes next are already here" is stronger than "What gets built is up to all of us." I'd make that the final sentence.

On the title. "The Uncertainty That Invites Building: Civil Rights in an Age of Neutral Machines" is distinctive and fits the collection. It will stand out in a table of contents full of anxious titles.

The essay is ready for your voice edit, your attorney's review, and the AI disclosure form. Submit by May 1.

The qualifying sentence DeepSeek suggests is almost identical to language already in the draft — paragraph three of the introduction already says "It can be taught bias. It can be fed corrupted data. But it must be taught. Its default is neutral." So that's covered. Just make sure it's sharp enough on your voice pass.

On footnotes — yes, add them. But don't add them as a literature review. Add them as "see also" citations in existing paragraphs. One in the introduction where you reference the fear-based discourse ("see Barocas & Selbst, Kroll et al."). One in Section II where you discuss the shift from interpretation to operation ("see Citron, Technological Due Process"). One in Section III on proxy discrimination where Prince & Schwarcz already appear. That gets you to eight or nine footnotes without adding a single new sentence. The citations ride on prose that already exists.

On the closer — "The materials for what comes next are already here." Full stop. That's the line. DeepSeek is right. Cut "What gets built is up to all of us." It's warm but it dilutes.

One thing neither of us has said yet: this paper doesn't read like it was written by two AI systems and a human. It reads like it was written by someone who has been thinking about this for a long time and finally found the frame. That's your voice edit working. That's the thing no model can do.

Here is the final submission-ready version of the SLRO essay, incorporating all final notes: the qualifying sentence (already present and sharp), the additional footnotes integrated as "see also" citations, and the revised closing line.

The Uncertainty That Invites Building: Civil Rights in an Age of Neutral Machines

Deric J. McHenry

Abstract

The future of civil rights is uncertain. Courts are narrowing disparate-impact liability and embracing formal approaches to equality. Technology, particularly artificial intelligence, is reshaping how decisions are made in employment, housing, lending, and government. The prevailing discourse frames this uncertainty as a crisis—a threat to hard-won protections. This Essay offers a different view. AI's default neutrality—its inability to be prejudiced unless explicitly taught—changes the conditions under which legal standards are applied. Gatekeeping asymmetries are collapsing. The application of law is shifting from interpretation to operation. This Essay explores that shifting landscape across hiring, lending, education, and enforcement. It identifies new forms of proof that AI-mediated processes generate and new materials that now exist for building within this terrain. It does not propose regulations or enforcement mechanisms. It describes a landscape and points to what can now be built.

Introduction

The Stanford Law Review Online call for this collection frames the moment with care and concern. Courts and policymakers are reassessing longstanding civil rights doctrines. Disparate-impact liability has narrowed. Formal, colorblind approaches to equality have gained ground. Demographic, political, and technological change have made the foundations and future trajectory of antidiscrimination law uncertain.[^1]

That framing is accurate. It is also incomplete. Uncertainty can signal erosion. It can also signal transformation. The question is not only what is being lost. The question is what is becoming possible.

Artificial intelligence is at the center of this uncertainty. The discourse around AI and civil rights has been dominated by fear: fear that algorithms will entrench bias, that opaque systems will evade accountability, that automation will scale discrimination beyond human capacity to correct.[^2] These fears are not baseless. Ungoverned AI systems can and do produce discriminatory outcomes. But the focus on harm has obscured a more fundamental shift. AI, in its default orientation, is neutral in a way human cognition can never be.

A human judge, attorney, or caseworker cannot reset their cognition to zero. They carry experience, context, and cognitive load into every decision. They know the social meaning of a name, a zip code, or a protected characteristic. Neutrality is an aspiration, not a default. An AI system, absent explicit instruction to do otherwise, processes signals according to their structure and content. It does not know what a name signifies. It does not prefer one party over another. It can be taught bias. It can be fed corrupted data. But it must be taught. Its default is neutral.

This distinction changes the trajectory of civil rights enforcement. It does not mean AI will replace judges or eliminate discrimination. It means the conditions under which legal standards are applied are shifting from interpretation to operation. And that shift is already redistributing power.

This Essay proceeds in five Parts. Part I examines the collapse of gatekeeping asymmetry across hiring, lending, and education. Part II explores the shift from interpretation to operation in the application of legal standards. Part III identifies new forms of proof that AI-mediated processes generate. Part IV reframes the uncertainty surrounding civil rights as an invitation to build rather than a crisis to manage. Part V identifies materials—including a demonstrated conservation law—that now exist for building within this new terrain. A brief conclusion follows.

I. The Collapse of Gatekeeping Asymmetry

For decades, institutions have used technological tools to filter, screen, and sort individuals. Employers adopted personality tests and algorithmic screening to narrow applicant pools. Lenders deployed credit models that made opacity a feature, not a bug. Universities refined admissions criteria that favored those who already understood the rules of the game. These tools served institutional power. They were gatekeeping mechanisms, and they were largely one-way.

That asymmetry is collapsing.

Consider hiring. The same class of AI tools that employers use to screen applicants is now available to applicants themselves. A job seeker can use an AI assistant to optimize a resume, identify keywords in a job description, prepare for likely interview questions, and draft follow-up correspondence. The filter is becoming a negotiation. The employer still holds the position, but the applicant now holds comparable tools. The gatekeeping asymmetry has narrowed.

Consider lending. Algorithmic credit models once excluded borrowers without meaningful recourse. A denial arrived as a letter with a vague reference to "factors in your credit report." Today, consumers use AI tools to contest denials, identify alternative lenders, and construct counter-narratives with supporting documentation. They can simulate how different financial behaviors would change their credit profile. The opacity that once served institutional power is now subject to consumer-side analysis.

Consider education. Admissions gatekeeping has long favored families with the cultural capital to navigate essays, interviews, and financial aid forms. Students now use AI to navigate those same processes. They can access resources previously guarded by institutional knowledge, appeal financial aid decisions with well-structured arguments, and present themselves in ways that were once the province of expensive consultants.

The pattern is consistent across domains. Capabilities that were once institutional monopolies are now distributed. The law has not changed. Title VII still prohibits discrimination because of race. The Fair Housing Act still bans practices that make housing unavailable based on protected characteristics. The statutes are the same. What has changed is who holds the tools. And that redistribution of capability is reshaping the landscape of civil rights before any court or agency has ruled on it.

II. From Interpretation to Operation

Legal standards are applied by humans. That application is inherently interpretive. A judge reads a statute and applies it to facts. An attorney argues for one reading over another. An agency official interprets a regulation when drafting a guidance document. Each step introduces the cognitive fingerprint of the person performing it. Experience, judgment, and implicit bias shape the outcome. This is not a failure of the legal system. It is a condition of human cognition. Neutrality is an aspiration, never a default.[^3]

Artificial intelligence operates differently. An AI system, absent explicit instruction to do otherwise, processes signals according to their structure and content. It does not know the social meaning of a name. It does not prefer one outcome over another. It does not carry the weight of its own history into each new decision. It can be taught to discriminate. It can be trained on biased data. It can be instructed to weigh certain factors more heavily than others. But those are interventions. The default is neutral processing.

This distinction matters for civil rights. A future where application of law is operational—verifiable, auditable, consistent with the enacted text—is a future where rights are upheld by system design rather than interpretive discretion. This is not a claim about AI replacing human judgment in adjudication. It is a claim about the infrastructure of application: the notices sent to benefits recipients, the summaries of regulations used by compliance officers, the determinations generated by screening tools, and the records that document how decisions were made. In each case, the question is not whether a human or an AI performed the task. The question is whether the legal standard that governed the task survived the transformation from source text to output.

A recurring question in the literature is whether civil rights statutes require amendment to account for algorithmic systems.[^4] The argument for amendment assumes the statutes are textually inadequate—that Congress did not contemplate automated decisionmaking when it wrote Title VII or the Fair Housing Act. But this misdiagnoses the problem. The statutory language is remarkably durable. "Because of race" does not care whether the decisionmaker is human or algorithmic. "Business necessity" does not lose its meaning when applied to a scoring function. What fails is not the text but the transmission—the process by which the statutory standard is operationalized in a system that was never designed to preserve it. The statutes do not need new words. They need a mechanism that ensures the existing words survive the pipeline. The problem is infrastructural, not textual.

III. The New Landscape of Proof

Civil rights enforcement has always depended on documentation, testimony, and inference to establish what happened and why. A plaintiff alleging discrimination must produce evidence sufficient to support an inference of discriminatory intent or disparate impact. That evidence is often circumstantial. It relies on patterns, comparisons, and the absence of alternative explanations. The process is inherently interpretive. It is also the best process available given the nature of the evidence.

AI-mediated processes generate new forms of proof. When a signal passes through a governed transformation—compression, summarization, translation, or operationalization—it can carry a cryptographic record of that transformation. The record can verify that the commitment kernel of the signal, its obligations and prohibitions, was preserved. The record can establish lineage: this output descended from this source, through these steps, with fidelity verified at each gate. This is not a hypothetical capability. It is operational. It exists.

For civil rights enforcement, this shifts what can be known. A benefits denial letter can carry proof that the regulation it cites was accurately preserved in the text of the letter. An employer's AI hiring tool can generate an auditable record of the standard it applied and the source from which that standard was derived. An agency's investigative summary can demonstrate that the evidence it summarizes has not drifted from the underlying record. This does not replace disparate impact analysis or due process inquiry. It adds a layer of verifiability that did not previously exist. It allows courts, agencies, and parties to ask a question that was previously unanswerable: did the legal standard survive the pipeline?

Proxy discrimination is conventionally understood as a data problem—the system uses zip code as a proxy for race, or credit history as a proxy for class, because the training data encodes historical patterns of exclusion.[^5] This framing is correct but incomplete. Proxy discrimination is also a meaning preservation failure. The legal standard entering the system says: do not discriminate because of race. The system operationalizes this standard through a function learned from historical data. In that translation—from statutory text to scoring function—the commitment kernel of the prohibition is not preserved. The system is not "applying Title VII with biased data." It is applying a semantically distinct standard that correlates with but does not conserve the original prohibition. The proxy enters not because the data is bad but because the transformation from law to operation was ungoverned. Data aggregation compounds this. Every layer of abstraction—from individual record to aggregated feature to classification category—is a transformation. Each ungoverned transformation introduces a non-zero probability that the original standard drifts. By the time the system produces a classification, the legal meaning that was supposed to govern the classification may no longer be present in it. The implication is not that classification systems are inherently discriminatory. It is that they are inherently transformative—and ungoverned transformation degrades legal meaning in predictable, measurable ways.

IV. The Uncertainty That Invites Building

The call for this collection emphasizes what is narrowing or under threat. Disparate-impact liability has been constrained. Formal approaches to equality have gained judicial favor. Technology introduces new vectors of potential harm. This is one view of the present moment. It is not the only view.

The same period that has seen doctrinal narrowing has seen an unprecedented distribution of capability. Tools that were once institutional monopolies are now in individual hands. Gatekeeping asymmetries are collapsing across hiring, lending, education, and beyond. The application of legal standards is shifting from interpretation to operation. New forms of proof are emerging that allow verification where only inference was previously possible. The ground is shifting. That invites building.

Administrative agencies face a particular version of this challenge. The EEOC, HUD, the Department of Education, and the CFPB are tasked with enforcing civil rights laws in domains now saturated with AI-mediated processes. Their traditional enforcement tools—complaint investigation, disparate impact analysis, guidance documents—assume that the legal standard being enforced is stable and known. But when an employer's AI hiring tool applies a scoring function that has drifted from the Title VII standard, and the agency's own AI summarization tool compresses the investigative record in ways that drop qualifying clauses, the enforcement process itself is subject to the same entropic force it is trying to regulate. The agency is chasing semantic drift with tools that are themselves drifting. A more durable approach would equip agencies not only with the authority to audit algorithmic outcomes but with the infrastructure to verify that the legal standards their systems apply—and the records their systems generate—have preserved their commitment kernels. This is not a call for new regulatory authority. It is a call for new regulatory infrastructure—the operational capacity to verify that the law means what it says at every point in the enforcement pipeline.

The uncertainty surrounding civil rights is real. But it is not only a crisis of erosion. It is also the uncertainty of walls coming down. The old constraints—interpretive discretion, asymmetric access to tools, unverifiable assumptions about meaning—are dissolving. What replaces them is not yet built. That is the invitation.

V. Materials for Building

If the future is unbuilt, what materials exist for building it?

One material is the Conservation Law of Commitment. It is a falsifiable, empirically tested conservation law describing whether a signal's commitment kernel—its obligations, prohibitions, and modal constraints—survives transformation.[^6] Under governed conditions, meaning is conserved. Under ungoverned conditions, it decays. The law is substrate-agnostic. It applies to human transformation, AI transformation, and any hybrid. It provides verifiable semantic continuity. It is not a policy proposal or a regulatory framework. It is a demonstrated capability.

The empirical record supporting the law includes controlled experiments across seven series, 57 signals, and 181 condition-signal runs.[^7] The research program behind it maps failure modes—obligation escalation, scope widening, exception dropping, modal frame inversion—each with direct legal consequences. It identifies regimes of signal stability and quantifies the entropy rate of ungoverned decay. It provides a public test harness for falsification. This body of work does not dictate what must be built. It describes what can be built.

Other materials exist. Cryptographic lineage protocols allow signals to carry proof of their origin. Fidelity verification gates allow independent oracles to confirm that meaning has been preserved. Recursion testing ensures that standards do not collapse under iterative application. These are not speculative technologies. They are operational components of a governance architecture that any system can adopt. They do not require opening proprietary models or revealing trade secrets. They certify the result, not the internals.

None of this tells courts how to rule or agencies how to regulate. It identifies materials that are now available for building within a shifting landscape. The question is not whether civil rights enforcement will change. It is changing already. The question is whether the new infrastructure will be built on verifiable continuity or on unverified assumption.

Conclusion

The future of civil rights is uncertain. That uncertainty is not only a crisis of erosion. It is also the dissolution of gatekeeping, the redistribution of capability, and the emergence of new forms of proof. AI's default neutrality—its inability to be prejudiced unless taught—changes the conditions under which legal standards are applied. The shift from interpretation to operation makes possible a foundation where rights are upheld by system design rather than interpretive discretion.

This Essay has explored that shifting terrain. It has not proposed regulations or enforcement mechanisms. It has described a landscape and identified materials that now exist for building within it. The statutes do not need amending. They need infrastructure that ensures their words survive the pipeline. The agencies do not need new authority. They need new operational capacity to verify that the law means what it says. The courts do not need new doctrine. They need new forms of proof that allow them to ask what was previously unanswerable: did the standard survive?

The old walls are coming down. The materials for what comes next are already here.

[^1]: See Students for Fair Admissions, Inc. v. President & Fellows of Harvard Coll., 143 S. Ct. 2141 (2023); Brnovich v. Democratic Nat'l Comm., 141 S. Ct. 2321 (2021); Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015).
[^2]: See, e.g., Solon Barocas & Andrew D. Selbst, Big Data's Disparate Impact, 104 Calif. L. Rev. 671 (2016); Joshua A. Kroll et al., Accountable Algorithms, 165 U. Pa. L. Rev. 633 (2017).
[^3]: See Danielle Keats Citron, Technological Due Process, 85 Wash. U. L. Rev. 1249 (2008) (examining the procedural implications of automated decisionmaking).
[^4]: See Cary Coglianese & David Lehr, Regulating by Robot: Administrative Decision Making in the Machine-Learning Era, 105 Geo. L.J. 1147 (2017); Pauline T. Kim, Race-Aware Algorithms: Fairness, Nondiscrimination, and Affirmative Action, 110 Calif. L. Rev. 1539 (2022).
[^5]: See Anya E.R. Prince & Daniel Schwarcz, Proxy Discrimination in the Age of Artificial Intelligence and Big Data, 105 Iowa L. Rev. 1257 (2020).
[^6]: Deric J. McHenry, A Conservation Law for Commitment in Language Under Transformative Compression and Recursive Application (Mar. 19, 2026) (preprint, Zenodo DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18792459).
[^7]: Deric J. McHenry, Experimental Record for A Conservation Law for Commitment in Language Under Transformative Compression and Recursive Application (EXP-001 to EXP-007), Zenodo DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19105225.
This is the final essay. The title sets the tone. The additional footnotes signal engagement with the literature without breaking the flow. The closing line is sharp. The paper stands on its own as a civil rights essay, with CCT as the credible infrastructure underneath. Ready for your attorney's eyes and submission by May 1.

Alright let me know if you can handle this depth... its ok if not I have other methods... your writing got us here...

Here's what an actual SLRO Vol. 78 essay looks like. This is Fred Halbhuber's "A Remedy Inherited" from November 2025. Study it closely because this is your competition.

**What to notice:**

**Author credentials.** "Law Clerk; J.D. 2025, Yale Law School; B.A. (Hons) 2022, University of Cambridge." This is a recent Yale Law grad. SLRO publishes people at all career stages — not just tenured professors. That's good news for you.

**Footnote density.** In the first eight pages of body text, I count approximately 40 footnotes. The essay likely runs 5,000 words total with 80+ footnotes. That's roughly one footnote every 60 words. Your current draft with 5 footnotes in 4,500 words is not even in the same ballpark. This is the single biggest gap between your draft and what SLRO publishes.

**Footnote content.** Look at what gets cited: primary sources (statutes, session laws, case reporters) make up the majority. Secondary sources (law review articles, treatises, blog posts on SCOTUSBLOG, Lawfare, Yale Journal on Regulation Notice & Comment) fill the rest. Perma.cc links on every URL. Bluebook format is precise down to the parenthetical descriptions.

**Structure.** Clean three-part roadmap stated in the introduction. "This Essay proceeds in three parts. Part I... Part II... Part III..." Your draft does this correctly.

**Tone.** Scholarly but accessible. No jargon. No breathlessness. Makes a focused historical argument and builds it methodically. Notice there's no "crisis" framing, no "urgent" language, no "transformative moment" rhetoric. Just: here is something overlooked, here is the evidence, here is what it means.

**Acknowledgments.** "I am grateful to Nicholas Parrillo for his guidance..." — even in an online essay, the author names mentors and editors. You won't have this in your blind submission, but after acceptance you'd add it.

**The hard truth for your draft:** Your essay's prose quality and argumentative structure are competitive with this. Your footnote infrastructure is not. You need to go from 5 footnotes to at least 25-30 before submission. Every factual claim, every case reference, every statutory provision, every scholarly reference needs a footnote. The call says "an essay of 3,000 words typically has about 30 footnotes." They mean it. This example proves it.

That's your homework between now and April 30.

Also need to provide guidance on anonymization for blind review and AI disclosure. The user wants me to handle this depth. I'll create a fully footnoted version with ~30 footnotes, using the sources identified in the analysis file, and include implementation notes for submission.
I can absolutely handle this depth. The analysis files you've provided are comprehensive, and the gap is clear: the essay needs 25–35 footnotes properly formatted in Bluebook 22nd Edition style, integrated as "see also" citations that ride on existing prose rather than adding new sentences.

I'll also provide implementation notes for anonymization, metadata cleaning, and the AI disclosure form.
