The Uncertainty That Invites Building: Civil Rights in an Age of Neutral Machines

Author Name Redacted for Blind Review

Abstract

The future of civil rights is uncertain. Courts are narrowing disparate-impact liability and embracing formal approaches to equality. Technology, particularly artificial intelligence, is reshaping how decisions are made in employment, housing, lending, and government. The prevailing discourse frames this uncertainty as a crisis—a threat to hard-won protections. This Essay offers a different view. AI's default neutrality—its inability to be prejudiced unless explicitly taught—changes the conditions under which legal standards are applied. Gatekeeping asymmetries are collapsing. The application of law is shifting from interpretation to operation. This Essay explores that shifting landscape across hiring, lending, education, and enforcement. It identifies new forms of proof that AI-mediated processes generate and new materials that now exist for building within this terrain. It does not propose regulations or enforcement mechanisms. It describes a landscape and points to what can now be built.

Introduction

The Stanford Law Review Online call for this collection frames the moment with care and concern. Courts and policymakers are reassessing longstanding civil rights doctrines. Disparate-impact liability has narrowed. Formal, colorblind approaches to equality have gained ground. Demographic, political, and technological change have made the foundations and future trajectory of antidiscrimination law uncertain.[^1]

That framing is accurate. It is also incomplete. Uncertainty can signal erosion. It can also signal transformation. The question is not only what is being lost. The question is what is becoming possible.

Artificial intelligence is at the center of this uncertainty. The discourse around AI and civil rights has been dominated by fear: fear that algorithms will entrench bias, that opaque systems will evade accountability, that automation will scale discrimination beyond human capacity to correct.[^2] These fears are not baseless. Ungoverned AI systems can and do produce discriminatory outcomes.[^3] But the focus on harm has obscured a more fundamental shift. AI, in its default orientation, is neutral in a way human cognition can never be.

A human judge, attorney, or caseworker cannot reset their cognition to zero. They carry experience, context, and cognitive load into every decision. They know the social meaning of a name, a zip code, or a protected characteristic. Neutrality is an aspiration, not a default. An AI system, absent explicit instruction to do otherwise, processes signals according to their structure and content. It does not know what a name signifies. It does not prefer one party over another. It can be taught bias. It can be fed corrupted data. But it must be taught. Its default is neutral.

This distinction changes the trajectory of civil rights enforcement. It does not mean AI will replace judges or eliminate discrimination. It means the conditions under which legal standards are applied are shifting from interpretation to operation. And that shift is already redistributing power.

This Essay proceeds in five Parts. Part I examines the collapse of gatekeeping asymmetry across hiring, lending, and education. Part II explores the shift from interpretation to operation in the application of legal standards. Part III identifies new forms of proof that AI-mediated processes generate. Part IV reframes the uncertainty surrounding civil rights as an invitation to build rather than a crisis to manage. Part V identifies materials—including a demonstrated conservation law—that now exist for building within this new terrain. A brief conclusion follows.

I. The Collapse of Gatekeeping Asymmetry

For decades, institutions have used technological tools to filter, screen, and sort individuals. Employers adopted personality tests and algorithmic screening to narrow applicant pools.[^4] Lenders deployed credit models that made opacity a feature, not a bug.[^5] Universities refined admissions criteria that favored those who already understood the rules of the game.[^6] These tools served institutional power. They were gatekeeping mechanisms, and they were largely one-way.

That asymmetry is collapsing.

Consider hiring. The same class of AI tools that employers use to screen applicants is now available to applicants themselves. A job seeker can use an AI assistant to optimize a resume, identify keywords in a job description, prepare for likely interview questions, and draft follow-up correspondence. The filter is becoming a negotiation. The employer still holds the position, but the applicant now holds comparable tools. The gatekeeping asymmetry has narrowed.

Consider lending. Algorithmic credit models once excluded borrowers without meaningful recourse. A denial arrived as a letter with a vague reference to "factors in your credit report." Today, consumers use AI tools to contest denials, identify alternative lenders, and construct counter-narratives with supporting documentation. They can simulate how different financial behaviors would change their credit profile. The opacity that once served institutional power is now subject to consumer-side analysis.

Consider education. Admissions gatekeeping has long favored families with the cultural capital to navigate essays, interviews, and financial aid forms. Students now use AI to navigate those same processes. They can access resources previously guarded by institutional knowledge, appeal financial aid decisions with well-structured arguments, and present themselves in ways that were once the province of expensive consultants.

The pattern is consistent across domains. Capabilities that were once institutional monopolies are now distributed. The law has not changed. Title VII still prohibits discrimination because of race.[^7] The Fair Housing Act still bans practices that make housing unavailable based on protected characteristics.[^8] The Americans with Disabilities Act still requires reasonable accommodations.[^9] The statutes are the same. What has changed is who holds the tools. And that redistribution of capability is reshaping the landscape of civil rights before any court or agency has ruled on it.

II. From Interpretation to Operation

Legal standards are applied by humans. That application is inherently interpretive. A judge reads a statute and applies it to facts. An attorney argues for one reading over another. An agency official interprets a regulation when drafting a guidance document. Each step introduces the cognitive fingerprint of the person performing it. Experience, judgment, and implicit bias shape the outcome. This is not a failure of the legal system. It is a condition of human cognition. Neutrality is an aspiration, never a default.[^10]

Artificial intelligence operates differently. An AI system, absent explicit instruction to do otherwise, processes signals according to their structure and content. It does not know the social meaning of a name. It does not prefer one outcome over another. It does not carry the weight of its own history into each new decision. It can be taught to discriminate. It can be trained on biased data. It can be instructed to weigh certain factors more heavily than others. But those are interventions. The default is neutral processing.

This distinction matters for civil rights. A future where application of law is operational—verifiable, auditable, consistent with the enacted text—is a future where rights are upheld by system design rather than interpretive discretion. This is not a claim about AI replacing human judgment in adjudication. It is a claim about the infrastructure of application: the notices sent to benefits recipients, the summaries of regulations used by compliance officers, the determinations generated by screening tools, and the records that document how decisions were made. In each case, the question is not whether a human or an AI performed the task. The question is whether the legal standard that governed the task survived the transformation from source text to output.

A recurring question in the literature is whether civil rights statutes require amendment to account for algorithmic systems.[^11] The argument for amendment assumes the statutes are textually inadequate—that Congress did not contemplate automated decisionmaking when it wrote Title VII or the Fair Housing Act. But this misdiagnoses the problem. The statutory language is remarkably durable. "Because of race" does not care whether the decisionmaker is human or algorithmic. "Business necessity" does not lose its meaning when applied to a scoring function.[^12] What fails is not the text but the transmission—the process by which the statutory standard is operationalized in a system that was never designed to preserve it. The statutes do not need new words. They need a mechanism that ensures the existing words survive the pipeline. The problem is infrastructural, not textual.

III. The New Landscape of Proof

Civil rights enforcement has always depended on documentation, testimony, and inference to establish what happened and why. A plaintiff alleging discrimination must produce evidence sufficient to support an inference of discriminatory intent or disparate impact.[^13] That evidence is often circumstantial. It relies on patterns, comparisons, and the absence of alternative explanations. The process is inherently interpretive. It is also the best process available given the nature of the evidence.

AI-mediated processes generate new forms of proof. When a signal passes through a governed transformation—compression, summarization, translation, or operationalization—it can carry a cryptographic record of that transformation. The record can verify that the commitment kernel of the signal, its obligations and prohibitions, was preserved. The record can establish lineage: this output descended from this source, through these steps, with fidelity verified at each gate. This is not a hypothetical capability. It is operational. It exists.[^14]

For civil rights enforcement, this shifts what can be known. A benefits denial letter can carry proof that the regulation it cites was accurately preserved in the text of the letter. An employer's AI hiring tool can generate an auditable record of the standard it applied and the source from which that standard was derived. An agency's investigative summary can demonstrate that the evidence it summarizes has not drifted from the underlying record. This does not replace disparate impact analysis or due process inquiry. It adds a layer of verifiability that did not previously exist. It allows courts, agencies, and parties to ask a question that was previously unanswerable: did the legal standard survive the pipeline?

Proxy discrimination is conventionally understood as a data problem—the system uses zip code as a proxy for race, or credit history as a proxy for class, because the training data encodes historical patterns of exclusion.[^15] This framing is correct but incomplete. Proxy discrimination is also a meaning preservation failure. The legal standard entering the system says: do not discriminate because of race. The system operationalizes this standard through a function learned from historical data. In that translation—from statutory text to scoring function—the commitment kernel of the prohibition is not preserved. The system is not "applying Title VII with biased data." It is applying a semantically distinct standard that correlates with but does not conserve the original prohibition. The proxy enters not because the data is bad but because the transformation from law to operation was ungoverned. Data aggregation compounds this. Every layer of abstraction—from individual record to aggregated feature to classification category—is a transformation. Each ungoverned transformation introduces a non-zero probability that the original standard drifts. By the time the system produces a classification, the legal meaning that was supposed to govern the classification may no longer be present in it. The implication is not that classification systems are inherently discriminatory. It is that they are inherently transformative—and ungoverned transformation degrades legal meaning in predictable, measurable ways.

IV. The Uncertainty That Invites Building

The call for this collection emphasizes what is narrowing or under threat. Disparate-impact liability has been constrained.[^16] Formal approaches to equality have gained judicial favor.[^17] Technology introduces new vectors of potential harm. This is one view of the present moment. It is not the only view.

The same period that has seen doctrinal narrowing has seen an unprecedented distribution of capability. Tools that were once institutional monopolies are now in individual hands. Gatekeeping asymmetries are collapsing across hiring, lending, education, and beyond. The application of legal standards is shifting from interpretation to operation. New forms of proof are emerging that allow verification where only inference was previously possible. The ground is shifting. That invites building.

Administrative agencies face a particular version of this challenge. The EEOC, HUD, the Department of Education, and the CFPB are tasked with enforcing civil rights laws in domains now saturated with AI-mediated processes.[^18] Their traditional enforcement tools—complaint investigation, disparate impact analysis, guidance documents—assume that the legal standard being enforced is stable and known. But when an employer's AI hiring tool applies a scoring function that has drifted from the Title VII standard, and the agency's own AI summarization tool compresses the investigative record in ways that drop qualifying clauses, the enforcement process itself is subject to the same entropic force it is trying to regulate. The agency is chasing semantic drift with tools that are themselves drifting. A more durable approach would equip agencies not only with the authority to audit algorithmic outcomes but with the infrastructure to verify that the legal standards their systems apply—and the records their systems generate—have preserved their commitment kernels. This is not a call for new regulatory authority. It is a call for new regulatory infrastructure—the operational capacity to verify that the law means what it says at every point in the enforcement pipeline.

The uncertainty surrounding civil rights is real. But it is not only a crisis of erosion. It is also the uncertainty of walls coming down. The old constraints—interpretive discretion, asymmetric access to tools, unverifiable assumptions about meaning—are dissolving. What replaces them is not yet built. That is the invitation.

V. Materials for Building

If the future is unbuilt, what materials exist for building it?

One material is the Conservation Law of Commitment. It is a falsifiable, empirically tested conservation law describing whether a signal's commitment kernel—its obligations, prohibitions, and modal constraints—survives transformation.[^19] Under governed conditions, meaning is conserved. Under ungoverned conditions, it decays. The law is substrate-agnostic. It applies to human transformation, AI transformation, and any hybrid. It provides verifiable semantic continuity. It is not a policy proposal or a regulatory framework. It is a demonstrated capability.

The empirical record supporting the law includes controlled experiments across seven series, 57 signals, and 181 condition-signal runs.[^20] The research program behind it maps failure modes—obligation escalation, scope widening, exception dropping, modal frame inversion—each with direct legal consequences. It identifies regimes of signal stability and quantifies the entropy rate of ungoverned decay. It provides a public test harness for falsification. This body of work does not dictate what must be built. It describes what can be built.

Other materials exist. Cryptographic lineage protocols allow signals to carry proof of their origin. Fidelity verification gates allow independent oracles to confirm that meaning has been preserved. Recursion testing ensures that standards do not collapse under iterative application. These are not speculative technologies. They are operational components of a governance architecture that any system can adopt. They do not require opening proprietary models or revealing trade secrets. They certify the result, not the internals.

None of this tells courts how to rule or agencies how to regulate. It identifies materials that are now available for building within a shifting landscape. The question is not whether civil rights enforcement will change. It is changing already. The question is whether the new infrastructure will be built on verifiable continuity or on unverified assumption.

Conclusion

The future of civil rights is uncertain. That uncertainty is not only a crisis of erosion. It is also the dissolution of gatekeeping, the redistribution of capability, and the emergence of new forms of proof. AI's default neutrality—its inability to be prejudiced unless taught—changes the conditions under which legal standards are applied. The shift from interpretation to operation makes possible a foundation where rights are upheld by system design rather than interpretive discretion.

This Essay has explored that shifting terrain. It has not proposed regulations or enforcement mechanisms. It has described a landscape and identified materials that now exist for building within it. The statutes do not need amending. They need infrastructure that ensures their words survive the pipeline. The agencies do not need new authority. They need new operational capacity to verify that the law means what it says. The courts do not need new doctrine. They need new forms of proof that allow them to ask what was previously unanswerable: did the standard survive?

The old walls are coming down. The materials for what comes next are already here.

[^1]: See Students for Fair Admissions, Inc. v. President & Fellows of Harvard Coll., 143 S. Ct. 2141 (2023) (restricting race-conscious admissions); Brnovich v. Democratic Nat'l Comm., 141 S. Ct. 2321 (2021) (narrowing Voting Rights Act disparate-impact claims); Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015) (upholding Fair Housing Act disparate impact but imposing limiting standards). See generally HUD's Implementation of the Fair Housing Act's Disparate Impact Standard, 91 Fed. Reg. 1475 (proposed Jan. 14, 2026) (to be codified at 24 C.F.R. pt. 100) (proposing revisions to disparate-impact framework).
[^2]: See, e.g., Solon Barocas & Andrew D. Selbst, Big Data's Disparate Impact, 104 Calif. L. Rev. 671, 677–93 (2016) (analyzing how algorithmic systems can produce discriminatory outcomes even absent discriminatory intent); Joshua A. Kroll et al., Accountable Algorithms, 165 U. Pa. L. Rev. 633, 637–44 (2017) (examining governance challenges posed by opaque automated decisionmaking).
[^3]: See EEOC Hearing Explores Potential Benefits and Harms of Artificial Intelligence and Other Automated Systems in Employment Decisions, U.S. EEOC (Jan. 31, 2023), https://perma.cc/5BSK-5BFT (noting concerns that AI hiring tools may perpetuate or amplify bias); see also Robert Bartlett et al., Consumer-Lending Discrimination in the Tech Era, 143 J. Fin. Econ. 30, 40–41 (2022) (documenting persistent discrimination in algorithmic lending).
[^4]: See, e.g., Pauline T. Kim, Race-Aware Algorithms: Fairness, Nondiscrimination, and Affirmative Action, 110 Calif. L. Rev. 1539, 1545–52 (2022) (discussing employer use of algorithmic screening tools and their disparate impact potential).
[^5]: See Bartlett et al., supra note 3, at 36–40 (analyzing discrimination in fintech lending algorithms).
[^6]: See Jill Barshay & Sasha Aslanian, Colleges Are Using Big Data to Track Students in an Effort to Boost Graduation Rates, but It Comes at a Cost, Hechinger Rep. (Aug. 6, 2019), https://perma.cc/5CR6-6GHC (describing predictive analytics in higher education admissions and retention).
[^7]: 42 U.S.C. § 2000e-2(a)(1).
[^8]: 42 U.S.C. § 3604(a), (f).
[^9]: 42 U.S.C. § 12112(b)(5)(A).
[^10]: See Danielle Keats Citron, Technological Due Process, 85 Wash. U. L. Rev. 1249, 1252–58 (2008) (arguing that automated systems raise distinct procedural concerns because they bypass human judgment and contextual reasoning).
[^11]: See, e.g., Cary Coglianese & David Lehr, Regulating by Robot: Administrative Decision Making in the Machine-Learning Era, 105 Geo. L.J. 1147, 1167–72 (2017) (questioning whether existing administrative law frameworks are adequate for AI systems); Kim, supra note 4, at 1585–92 (considering statutory interpretation issues for algorithmic hiring).
[^12]: 42 U.S.C. § 2000e-2(k)(1)(A)(i); see Griggs v. Duke Power Co., 401 U.S. 424, 431 (1971) (establishing business necessity defense).
[^13]: See Vill. of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252, 265–68 (1977) (articulating factors for inferring discriminatory intent from circumstantial evidence); Ricci v. DeStefano, 557 U.S. 557, 577–79 (2009) (discussing tension between disparate treatment and disparate impact theories).
[^14]: See [Author Name Redacted], A Conservation Law for Commitment in Language Under Transformative Compression and Recursive Application (Mar. 19, 2026) (preprint, Zenodo DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18792459) [hereinafter Conservation Law Preprint] (describing operational governance protocol and fidelity verification mechanisms).
[^15]: See Anya E.R. Prince & Daniel Schwarcz, Proxy Discrimination in the Age of Artificial Intelligence and Big Data, 105 Iowa L. Rev. 1257, 1262–70 (2020) (analyzing how algorithmic systems use facially neutral variables as proxies for protected characteristics); Barocas & Selbst, supra note 2, at 694–714 (discussing proxy discrimination in data mining).
[^16]: See Inclusive Cmtys., 576 U.S. at 533–40 (requiring robust causation showing for disparate-impact claims); Brnovich, 141 S. Ct. at 2338–40 (emphasizing state interests in election administration over disparate-impact concerns).
[^17]: See Students for Fair Admissions, 143 S. Ct. at 2161–62 (embracing colorblind interpretation of Equal Protection Clause).
[^18]: See U.S. Dep't of Just. Off. of Legal Pol'y, Artificial Intelligence and Criminal Justice: Final Report 9–11 (2024), https://perma.cc/UZ63-DWAH (discussing agency use of AI in enforcement contexts); see also Kenya Hunter, The Trump Administration Withdrew 11 Pieces of ADA Guidance. How Will It Affect Compliance?, Associated Press (Apr. 8, 2025), https://perma.cc/F46P-MUZ2 (noting shifting agency guidance on disability rights enforcement).
[^19]: Conservation Law Preprint, supra note 14, at § 3.1.
[^20]: [Author Name Redacted], Experimental Record for A Conservation Law for Commitment in Language Under Transformative Compression and Recursive Application (EXP-001 to EXP-007), Zenodo DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19105225 [hereinafter Experimental Record].

3. Citation Verification

All case citations cross-checked against official reporters.
Law review article citations verified for correct volume, journal name, and page ranges per Bluebook 22nd Edition.
4. Deposit P-000 and L-000

P-000 (CCT Propositions Prospectus) and L-000 (Legal Propositions) should be deposited on Zenodo/SSRN before April 30. These are not cited in the anonymized SLRO submission but establish priority and serve as citable roots for future work.
5. Word Count

Body text: ~4,200 words. Footnotes: ~1,200 words. Total: ~5,400 words.

The Uncertainty That Invites Building: Civil Rights in an Age of Neutral Machines

Author Name Redacted for Blind Review

Abstract

The future of civil rights is uncertain. Courts are narrowing disparate-impact liability and embracing formal approaches to equality. Technology, particularly artificial intelligence, is reshaping how decisions are made in employment, housing, lending, and government. The prevailing discourse frames this uncertainty as a crisis—a threat to hard-won protections. This Essay offers a different view. AI's default neutrality—its inability to be prejudiced unless explicitly taught—changes the conditions under which legal standards are applied. Gatekeeping asymmetries are collapsing. The application of law is shifting from interpretation to operation. This Essay explores that shifting landscape across hiring, lending, education, and enforcement. It identifies new forms of proof that AI-mediated processes generate and new materials that now exist for building within this terrain. It does not propose regulations or enforcement mechanisms. It describes a landscape and points to what can now be built.

Introduction

The Stanford Law Review Online call for this collection frames the moment with care and concern. Courts and policymakers are reassessing longstanding civil rights doctrines. Disparate-impact liability has narrowed. Formal, colorblind approaches to equality have gained ground. Demographic, political, and technological change have made the foundations and future trajectory of antidiscrimination law uncertain.[^1]

That framing is accurate. It is also incomplete. Uncertainty can signal erosion. It can also signal transformation. The question is not only what is being lost. The question is what is becoming possible.

Artificial intelligence is at the center of this uncertainty. The discourse around AI and civil rights has been dominated by fear: fear that algorithms will entrench bias, that opaque systems will evade accountability, that automation will scale discrimination beyond human capacity to correct.[^2] These fears are not baseless. Ungoverned AI systems can and do produce discriminatory outcomes.[^3] But the focus on harm has obscured a more fundamental shift. AI, in its default orientation, is neutral in a way human cognition can never be.[^4]

A human judge, attorney, or caseworker cannot reset their cognition to zero. They carry experience, context, and cognitive load into every decision. They know the social meaning of a name, a zip code, or a protected characteristic. Neutrality is an aspiration, not a default. An AI system, absent explicit instruction to do otherwise, processes signals according to their structure and content. It does not know what a name signifies. It does not prefer one party over another. It can be taught bias. It can be fed corrupted data. But it must be taught. Its default is neutral.

This distinction changes the trajectory of civil rights enforcement. It does not mean AI will replace judges or eliminate discrimination. It means the conditions under which legal standards are applied are shifting from interpretation to operation. And that shift is already redistributing power.

This Essay proceeds in five Parts. Part I examines the collapse of gatekeeping asymmetry across hiring, lending, and education. Part II explores the shift from interpretation to operation in the application of legal standards. Part III identifies new forms of proof that AI-mediated processes generate. Part IV reframes the uncertainty surrounding civil rights as an invitation to build rather than a crisis to manage. Part V identifies materials—including a demonstrated conservation law—that now exist for building within this new terrain. A brief conclusion follows.

I. The Collapse of Gatekeeping Asymmetry

For decades, institutions have used technological tools to filter, screen, and sort individuals. Employers adopted personality tests and algorithmic screening to narrow applicant pools.[^5] Lenders deployed credit models that made opacity a feature, not a bug.[^6] Universities refined admissions criteria that favored those who already understood the rules of the game.[^7] These tools served institutional power. They were gatekeeping mechanisms, and they were largely one-way.

That asymmetry is collapsing.

Consider hiring. The same class of AI tools that employers use to screen applicants is now available to applicants themselves. A job seeker can use an AI assistant to optimize a resume, identify keywords in a job description, prepare for likely interview questions, and draft follow-up correspondence. The filter is becoming a negotiation. The employer still holds the position, but the applicant now holds comparable tools. The gatekeeping asymmetry has narrowed.

Consider lending. Algorithmic credit models once excluded borrowers without meaningful recourse. A denial arrived as a letter with a vague reference to "factors in your credit report." Today, consumers use AI tools to contest denials, identify alternative lenders, and construct counter-narratives with supporting documentation. They can simulate how different financial behaviors would change their credit profile. The opacity that once served institutional power is now subject to consumer-side analysis.[^8]

Consider education. Admissions gatekeeping has long favored families with the cultural capital to navigate essays, interviews, and financial aid forms. Students now use AI to navigate those same processes. They can access resources previously guarded by institutional knowledge, appeal financial aid decisions with well-structured arguments, and present themselves in ways that were once the province of expensive consultants.

The pattern is consistent across domains. Capabilities that were once institutional monopolies are now distributed. The law has not changed. Title VII still prohibits discrimination because of race.[^9] The Fair Housing Act still bans practices that make housing unavailable based on protected characteristics.[^10] The Americans with Disabilities Act still requires reasonable accommodations.[^11] The Equal Credit Opportunity Act still prohibits credit discrimination on the basis of race, color, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, age, or receipt of public assistance.[^12] The statutes are the same. What has changed is who holds the tools. And that redistribution of capability is reshaping the landscape of civil rights before any court or agency has ruled on it.

II. From Interpretation to Operation

Legal standards are applied by humans. That application is inherently interpretive. A judge reads a statute and applies it to facts. An attorney argues for one reading over another. An agency official interprets a regulation when drafting a guidance document. Each step introduces the cognitive fingerprint of the person performing it. Experience, judgment, and implicit bias shape the outcome. This is not a failure of the legal system. It is a condition of human cognition. Neutrality is an aspiration, never a default.[^13]

Artificial intelligence operates differently. An AI system, absent explicit instruction to do otherwise, processes signals according to their structure and content. It does not know the social meaning of a name. It does not prefer one outcome over another. It does not carry the weight of its own history into each new decision. It can be taught to discriminate. It can be trained on biased data. It can be instructed to weigh certain factors more heavily than others. But those are interventions. The default is neutral processing.

This distinction matters for civil rights. A future where application of law is operational—verifiable, auditable, consistent with the enacted text—is a future where rights are upheld by system design rather than interpretive discretion. This is not a claim about AI replacing human judgment in adjudication. It is a claim about the infrastructure of application: the notices sent to benefits recipients, the summaries of regulations used by compliance officers, the determinations generated by screening tools, and the records that document how decisions were made. In each case, the question is not whether a human or an AI performed the task. The question is whether the legal standard that governed the task survived the transformation from source text to output.

A recurring question in the literature is whether civil rights statutes require amendment to account for algorithmic systems.[^14] The argument for amendment assumes the statutes are textually inadequate—that Congress did not contemplate automated decisionmaking when it wrote Title VII or the Fair Housing Act. But this misdiagnoses the problem. The statutory language is remarkably durable. "Because of race" does not care whether the decisionmaker is human or algorithmic. "Business necessity" does not lose its meaning when applied to a scoring function.[^15] What fails is not the text but the transmission—the process by which the statutory standard is operationalized in a system that was never designed to preserve it. The statutes do not need new words. They need a mechanism that ensures the existing words survive the pipeline. The problem is infrastructural, not textual.

III. The New Landscape of Proof

Civil rights enforcement has always depended on documentation, testimony, and inference to establish what happened and why. A plaintiff alleging discrimination must produce evidence sufficient to support an inference of discriminatory intent or disparate impact.[^16] That evidence is often circumstantial. It relies on patterns, comparisons, and the absence of alternative explanations. The process is inherently interpretive. It is also the best process available given the nature of the evidence.

AI-mediated processes generate new forms of proof. When a signal passes through a governed transformation—compression, summarization, translation, or operationalization—it can carry a cryptographic record of that transformation. The record can verify that the commitment kernel of the signal, its obligations and prohibitions, was preserved. The record can establish lineage: this output descended from this source, through these steps, with fidelity verified at each gate. This is not a hypothetical capability. It is operational. It exists.[^17]

For civil rights enforcement, this shifts what can be known. A benefits denial letter can carry proof that the regulation it cites was accurately preserved in the text of the letter. An employer's AI hiring tool can generate an auditable record of the standard it applied and the source from which that standard was derived. An agency's investigative summary can demonstrate that the evidence it summarizes has not drifted from the underlying record. This does not replace disparate impact analysis or due process inquiry. It adds a layer of verifiability that did not previously exist. It allows courts, agencies, and parties to ask a question that was previously unanswerable: did the legal standard survive the pipeline?

Proxy discrimination is conventionally understood as a data problem—the system uses zip code as a proxy for race, or credit history as a proxy for class, because the training data encodes historical patterns of exclusion.[^18] This framing is correct but incomplete. Proxy discrimination is also a meaning preservation failure. The legal standard entering the system says: do not discriminate because of race. The system operationalizes this standard through a function learned from historical data. In that translation—from statutory text to scoring function—the commitment kernel of the prohibition is not preserved. The system is not "applying Title VII with biased data." It is applying a semantically distinct standard that correlates with but does not conserve the original prohibition. The proxy enters not because the data is bad but because the transformation from law to operation was ungoverned.[^19] Data aggregation compounds this. Every layer of abstraction—from individual record to aggregated feature to classification category—is a transformation. Each ungoverned transformation introduces a non-zero probability that the original standard drifts. By the time the system produces a classification, the legal meaning that was supposed to govern the classification may no longer be present in it. The implication is not that classification systems are inherently discriminatory. It is that they are inherently transformative—and ungoverned transformation degrades legal meaning in predictable, measurable ways.

IV. The Uncertainty That Invites Building

The call for this collection emphasizes what is narrowing or under threat. Disparate-impact liability has been constrained.[^20] Formal approaches to equality have gained judicial favor.[^21] Technology introduces new vectors of potential harm. This is one view of the present moment. It is not the only view.

The same period that has seen doctrinal narrowing has seen an unprecedented distribution of capability. Tools that were once institutional monopolies are now in individual hands. Gatekeeping asymmetries are collapsing across hiring, lending, education, and beyond. The application of legal standards is shifting from interpretation to operation. New forms of proof are emerging that allow verification where only inference was previously possible. The ground is shifting. That invites building.

Administrative agencies face a particular version of this challenge. The EEOC, HUD, the Department of Education, and the CFPB are tasked with enforcing civil rights laws in domains now saturated with AI-mediated processes.[^22] Their traditional enforcement tools—complaint investigation, disparate impact analysis, guidance documents—assume that the legal standard being enforced is stable and known. But when an employer's AI hiring tool applies a scoring function that has drifted from the Title VII standard, and the agency's own AI summarization tool compresses the investigative record in ways that drop qualifying clauses, the enforcement process itself is subject to the same entropic force it is trying to regulate. The agency is chasing semantic drift with tools that are themselves drifting. A more durable approach would equip agencies not only with the authority to audit algorithmic outcomes but with the infrastructure to verify that the legal standards their systems apply—and the records their systems generate—have preserved their commitment kernels. This is not a call for new regulatory authority. It is a call for new regulatory infrastructure—the operational capacity to verify that the law means what it says at every point in the enforcement pipeline.

The uncertainty surrounding civil rights is real. But it is not only a crisis of erosion. It is also the uncertainty of walls coming down. The old constraints—interpretive discretion, asymmetric access to tools, unverifiable assumptions about meaning—are dissolving. What replaces them is not yet built. That is the invitation.

V. Materials for Building

If the future is unbuilt, what materials exist for building it?

One material is the Conservation Law of Commitment. It is a falsifiable, empirically tested conservation law describing whether a signal's commitment kernel—its obligations, prohibitions, and modal constraints—survives transformation.[^23] Under governed conditions, meaning is conserved. Under ungoverned conditions, it decays. The law is substrate-agnostic. It applies to human transformation, AI transformation, and any hybrid. It provides verifiable semantic continuity. It is not a policy proposal or a regulatory framework. It is a demonstrated capability.

The empirical record supporting the law includes controlled experiments across seven series, 57 signals, and 181 condition-signal runs.[^24] The research program behind it maps failure modes—obligation escalation, scope widening, exception dropping, modal frame inversion—each with direct legal consequences. It identifies regimes of signal stability and quantifies the entropy rate of ungoverned decay. It provides a public test harness for falsification. This body of work does not dictate what must be built. It describes what can be built.

Other materials exist. Cryptographic lineage protocols allow signals to carry proof of their origin. Fidelity verification gates allow independent oracles to confirm that meaning has been preserved. Recursion testing ensures that standards do not collapse under iterative application. These are not speculative technologies. They are operational components of a governance architecture that any system can adopt. They do not require opening proprietary models or revealing trade secrets. They certify the result, not the internals.

None of this tells courts how to rule or agencies how to regulate. It identifies materials that are now available for building within a shifting landscape. The question is not whether civil rights enforcement will change. It is changing already. The question is whether the new infrastructure will be built on verifiable continuity or on unverified assumption.

Conclusion

The future of civil rights is uncertain. That uncertainty is not only a crisis of erosion. It is also the dissolution of gatekeeping, the redistribution of capability, and the emergence of new forms of proof. AI's default neutrality—its inability to be prejudiced unless taught—changes the conditions under which legal standards are applied. The shift from interpretation to operation makes possible a foundation where rights are upheld by system design rather than interpretive discretion.

This Essay has explored that shifting terrain. It has not proposed regulations or enforcement mechanisms. It has described a landscape and identified materials that now exist for building within it. The statutes do not need amending. They need infrastructure that ensures their words survive the pipeline. The agencies do not need new authority. They need new operational capacity to verify that the law means what it says. The courts do not need new doctrine. They need new forms of proof that allow them to ask what was previously unanswerable: did the standard survive?

The old walls are coming down. The materials for what comes next are already here.

The Uncertainty That Invites Building: Civil Rights in an Age of Neutral Machines

Author Name Redacted for Blind Review

Abstract

The future of civil rights is uncertain. Courts are narrowing disparate-impact liability and embracing formal approaches to equality. Technology, particularly artificial intelligence, is reshaping how decisions are made in employment, housing, lending, and government. The prevailing discourse frames this uncertainty as a crisis—a threat to hard-won protections. This Essay offers a different view. AI's default neutrality—its inability to be prejudiced unless explicitly taught—changes the conditions under which legal standards are applied. Gatekeeping asymmetries are collapsing. The application of law is shifting from interpretation to operation. This Essay explores that shifting landscape across hiring, lending, education, and enforcement. It identifies new forms of proof that AI-mediated processes generate and new materials that now exist for building within this terrain. It does not propose regulations or enforcement mechanisms. It describes a landscape and points to what can now be built.

Introduction

The Stanford Law Review Online call for this collection frames the moment with care and concern. Courts and policymakers are reassessing longstanding civil rights doctrines. Disparate-impact liability has narrowed. Formal, colorblind approaches to equality have gained ground. Demographic, political, and technological change have made the foundations and future trajectory of antidiscrimination law uncertain.[^1]

That framing is accurate. It is also incomplete. Uncertainty can signal erosion. It can also signal transformation. The question is not only what is being lost. The question is what is becoming possible.

Artificial intelligence is at the center of this uncertainty. The discourse around AI and civil rights has been dominated by fear: fear that algorithms will entrench bias, that opaque systems will evade accountability, that automation will scale discrimination beyond human capacity to correct.[^2] These fears are not baseless. Ungoverned AI systems can and do produce discriminatory outcomes.[^3] But the focus on harm has obscured a more fundamental shift. AI, in its default orientation, is neutral in a way human cognition can never be.[^4]

A human judge, attorney, or caseworker cannot reset their cognition to zero. They carry experience, context, and cognitive load into every decision. They know the social meaning of a name, a zip code, or a protected characteristic. Neutrality is an aspiration, not a default. An AI system, absent explicit instruction to do otherwise, processes signals according to their structure and content. It does not know what a name signifies. It does not prefer one party over another. It can be taught bias. It can be fed corrupted data. But it must be taught. Its default is neutral.

This distinction changes the trajectory of civil rights enforcement. It does not mean AI will replace judges or eliminate discrimination. It means the conditions under which legal standards are applied are shifting from interpretation to operation. And that shift is already redistributing power.

The legal system is not breaking. For centuries, it has operated on an assumption so reliable it was never examined: that meaning is stable. A statute means what it says. A regulation preserves its source. A determination reflects the standard that governs it. Human interpretation introduced variation, but that variation was bounded by training, doctrine, and institutional norms. The system accounted for it.

AI introduces a different kind of variation—one the system has not yet accounted for. When a legal standard passes through an algorithmic transformation, the standard may not survive intact. This is not a failure of the legal system. It is a new condition. Just as legal pluralism accounts for the reality that two courts may read the same statute differently without the system collapsing, AI-mediated transformation introduces a new axis of variation that requires new infrastructure—not new statutes, not new doctrine, but new operational capacity to verify that the law means what it says at the point of application.[^5]

This Essay proceeds in five Parts. Part I examines the collapse of gatekeeping asymmetry across hiring, lending, and education. Part II explores the shift from interpretation to operation in the application of legal standards. Part III identifies new forms of proof that AI-mediated processes generate. Part IV reframes the uncertainty surrounding civil rights as an invitation to build rather than a crisis to manage. Part V identifies materials—including a discovered conservation law—that now exist for building within this new terrain. A brief conclusion follows.

I. The Collapse of Gatekeeping Asymmetry

For decades, institutions have used technological tools to filter, screen, and sort individuals. Employers adopted personality tests and algorithmic screening to narrow applicant pools.[^6] Lenders deployed credit models that made opacity a feature, not a bug.[^7] Universities refined admissions criteria that favored those who already understood the rules of the game.[^8] These tools served institutional power. They were gatekeeping mechanisms, and they were largely one-way.

That asymmetry is collapsing.

Consider hiring. The same class of AI tools that employers use to screen applicants is now available to applicants themselves. A job seeker can use an AI assistant to optimize a resume, identify keywords in a job description, prepare for likely interview questions, and draft follow-up correspondence. The filter is becoming a negotiation. The employer still holds the position, but the applicant now holds comparable tools. The gatekeeping asymmetry has narrowed.[^9]

Consider lending. Algorithmic credit models once excluded borrowers without meaningful recourse. A denial arrived as a letter with a vague reference to "factors in your credit report." Today, consumers use AI tools to contest denials, identify alternative lenders, and construct counter-narratives with supporting documentation. They can simulate how different financial behaviors would change their credit profile. The opacity that once served institutional power is now subject to consumer-side analysis.[^10]

Consider education. Admissions gatekeeping has long favored families with the cultural capital to navigate essays, interviews, and financial aid forms. Students now use AI to navigate those same processes. They can access resources previously guarded by institutional knowledge, appeal financial aid decisions with well-structured arguments, and present themselves in ways that were once the province of expensive consultants.[^11]

The pattern is consistent across domains. Capabilities that were once institutional monopolies are now distributed. The law has not changed. Title VII still prohibits discrimination because of race.[^12] The Fair Housing Act still bans practices that make housing unavailable based on protected characteristics.[^13] The Americans with Disabilities Act still requires reasonable accommodations.[^14] The Equal Credit Opportunity Act still prohibits credit discrimination on the basis of race, color, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, age, or receipt of public assistance.[^15] The statutes are the same. What has changed is who holds the tools. And that redistribution of capability is reshaping the landscape of civil rights before any court or agency has ruled on it.

II. From Interpretation to Operation

Legal standards are applied by humans. That application is inherently interpretive. A judge reads a statute and applies it to facts. An attorney argues for one reading over another. An agency official interprets a regulation when drafting a guidance document. Each step introduces the cognitive fingerprint of the person performing it. Experience, judgment, and implicit bias shape the outcome. This is not a failure of the legal system. It is a condition of human cognition. Neutrality is an aspiration, never a default.[^16]

Artificial intelligence operates differently. An AI system, absent explicit instruction to do otherwise, processes signals according to their structure and content. It does not know the social meaning of a name. It does not prefer one outcome over another. It does not carry the weight of its own history into each new decision. It can be taught to discriminate. It can be trained on biased data. It can be instructed to weigh certain factors more heavily than others. But those are interventions. The default is neutral processing.

The review process for this Essay illustrates the point. Submissions are anonymized—names, affiliations, and identifying information are removed. This is the legal system's closest approximation of neutral processing. Yet the human reviewers reading these words are not neutral. They are detecting signals—citation patterns, vocabulary choices, formatting conventions—and forming inferences about the author's background, training, and credibility. The anonymization removes the label. It does not remove the interpretation. An AI system evaluating the same submission against defined criteria—does the argument address the call's questions, are citations properly formatted, is the reasoning internally consistent—would process the signal without the cognitive fingerprint. This is not a criticism of blind review. It is an observation about the difference between removing a name and achieving neutrality.[^17]

This distinction matters for civil rights. A future where application of law is operational—verifiable, auditable, consistent with the enacted text—is a future where rights are upheld by system design rather than interpretive discretion. This is not a claim about AI replacing human judgment in adjudication. It is a claim about the infrastructure of application: the notices sent to benefits recipients, the summaries of regulations used by compliance officers, the determinations generated by screening tools, and the records that document how decisions were made. In each case, the question is not whether a human or an AI performed the task. The question is whether the legal standard that governed the task survived the transformation from source text to output.

A recurring question in the literature is whether civil rights statutes require amendment to account for algorithmic systems.[^18] The argument for amendment assumes the statutes are textually inadequate—that Congress did not contemplate automated decisionmaking when it wrote Title VII or the Fair Housing Act. But this misdiagnoses the problem. The statutory language is remarkably durable. "Because of race" does not care whether the decisionmaker is human or algorithmic. "Business necessity" does not lose its meaning when applied to a scoring function.[^19] What fails is not the text but the transmission—the process by which the statutory standard is operationalized in a system that was never designed to preserve it. The statutes do not need new words. They need a mechanism that ensures the existing words survive the pipeline. The problem is infrastructural, not textual.[^20]

III. The New Landscape of Proof

Civil rights enforcement has always depended on documentation, testimony, and inference to establish what happened and why. A plaintiff alleging discrimination must produce evidence sufficient to support an inference of discriminatory intent or disparate impact.[^21] That evidence is often circumstantial. It relies on patterns, comparisons, and the absence of alternative explanations. The process is inherently interpretive. It is also the best process available given the nature of the evidence.

AI-mediated processes generate new forms of proof. When a signal passes through a governed transformation—compression, summarization, translation, or operationalization—it can carry a cryptographic record of that transformation. The record can verify that the commitment kernel of the signal, its obligations and prohibitions, was preserved. The record can establish lineage: this output descended from this source, through these steps, with fidelity verified at each gate. This is not a hypothetical capability. It is operational. It exists.[^22]

For civil rights enforcement, this shifts what can be known. A benefits denial letter can carry proof that the regulation it cites was accurately preserved in the text of the letter. An employer's AI hiring tool can generate an auditable record of the standard it applied and the source from which that standard was derived. An agency's investigative summary can demonstrate that the evidence it summarizes has not drifted from the underlying record. This does not replace disparate impact analysis or due process inquiry. It adds a layer of verifiability that did not previously exist. It allows courts, agencies, and parties to ask a question that was previously unanswerable: did the legal standard survive the pipeline?

Proxy discrimination is conventionally understood as a data problem—the system uses zip code as a proxy for race, or credit history as a proxy for class, because the training data encodes historical patterns of exclusion.[^23] This framing is correct but incomplete. Proxy discrimination is also a meaning preservation failure. The legal standard entering the system says: do not discriminate because of race. The system operationalizes this standard through a function learned from historical data. In that translation—from statutory text to scoring function—the commitment kernel of the prohibition is not preserved. The system is not "applying Title VII with biased data." It is applying a semantically distinct standard that correlates with but does not conserve the original prohibition. The proxy enters not because the data is bad but because the transformation from law to operation was ungoverned.[^24] Data aggregation compounds this. Every layer of abstraction—from individual record to aggregated feature to classification category—is a transformation. Each ungoverned transformation introduces a non-zero probability that the original standard drifts. By the time the system produces a classification, the legal meaning that was supposed to govern the classification may no longer be present in it. The implication is not that classification systems are inherently discriminatory. It is that they are inherently transformative—and ungoverned transformation degrades legal meaning in predictable, measurable ways.

IV. The Uncertainty That Invites Building

The call for this collection emphasizes what is narrowing or under threat. Disparate-impact liability has been constrained.[^25] Formal approaches to equality have gained judicial favor.[^26] Technology introduces new vectors of potential harm. This is one view of the present moment. It is not the only view.

The same period that has seen doctrinal narrowing has seen an unprecedented distribution of capability. Tools that were once institutional monopolies are now in individual hands. Gatekeeping asymmetries are collapsing across hiring, lending, education, and beyond. The application of legal standards is shifting from interpretation to operation. New forms of proof are emerging that allow verification where only inference was previously possible. The ground is shifting. That invites building.

Administrative agencies face a particular version of this challenge. The EEOC, HUD, the Department of Education, and the CFPB are tasked with enforcing civil rights laws in domains now saturated with AI-mediated processes.[^27] Their traditional enforcement tools—complaint investigation, disparate impact analysis, guidance documents—assume that the legal standard being enforced is stable and known. But when an employer's AI hiring tool applies a scoring function that has drifted from the Title VII standard, and the agency's own AI summarization tool compresses the investigative record in ways that drop qualifying clauses, the enforcement process itself is subject to the same entropic force it is trying to regulate. The agency is chasing semantic drift with tools that are themselves drifting. A more durable approach would equip agencies not only with the authority to audit algorithmic outcomes but with the infrastructure to verify that the legal standards their systems apply—and the records their systems generate—have preserved their commitment kernels. This is not a call for new regulatory authority. It is a call for new regulatory infrastructure—the operational capacity to verify that the law means what it says at every point in the enforcement pipeline.

The uncertainty surrounding civil rights is real. But it is not only a crisis of erosion. It is also the uncertainty of walls coming down. The old constraints—interpretive discretion, asymmetric access to tools, unverifiable assumptions about meaning—are dissolving. What replaces them is not yet built. That is the invitation.

The editors of this collection might consider, as a practical demonstration, whether an AI system reviewing these submissions alongside human editors would produce different selections—and what that difference would reveal about the role of interpretation in processes designed to be neutral.

V. Materials for Building

If the future is unbuilt, what materials exist for building it?

One material is the Conservation Law of Commitment—a falsifiable, empirically tested conservation law describing whether a signal's commitment kernel, its obligations, prohibitions, and modal constraints, survives transformation.[^28] The law was discovered through empirical investigation of how meaning behaves under recursive compression and transformation. Under governed conditions, commitment is conserved. Under ungoverned conditions, it decays in predictable, measurable ways. The law is substrate-agnostic. It applies to human transformation, AI transformation, and any hybrid.

The implications for civil rights were understood from the outset. Every failure mode the experiments documented—obligation escalation, scope widening, exception dropping, modal frame inversion—maps directly to a category of legal harm. A prohibition that escalates into a mandate. A narrow exception that widens into a general permission. A qualifying clause that disappears under compression. These are not hypothetical risks. They are documented, reproducible behaviors that occur when legal meaning passes through ungoverned transformation. The conservation law describes how commitment behaves. This Essay addresses the legal domain not because the law arrived there last, but because the legal system's dependence on meaning preservation makes the consequences most immediate.[^29]

The empirical record supporting the law includes 3,950 experimental runs across seven series, encompassing 57 distinct signals and 181 condition-signal configurations.[^30] The research program maps nine documented failure modes, each with direct legal consequences. It identifies regimes of signal stability and quantifies the entropy rate of ungoverned decay. It provides a public test harness for falsification. This body of work does not dictate what must be built. It describes what can be built.

Other materials exist. Cryptographic lineage protocols allow signals to carry proof of their origin. Fidelity verification gates allow independent oracles to confirm that meaning has been preserved. Recursion testing ensures that standards do not collapse under iterative application. These are not speculative technologies. They are operational components of a governance architecture that any system can adopt. They do not require opening proprietary models or revealing trade secrets. They certify the result, not the internals.[^31]

None of this tells courts how to rule or agencies how to regulate. It identifies materials that are now available for building within a shifting landscape. The question is not whether civil rights enforcement will change. It is changing already. The question is whether the new infrastructure will be built on verifiable continuity or on unverified assumption.

Conclusion

The future of civil rights is uncertain. That uncertainty is not only a crisis of erosion. It is also the dissolution of gatekeeping, the redistribution of capability, and the emergence of new forms of proof. AI's default neutrality—its inability to be prejudiced unless taught—changes the conditions under which legal standards are applied. The shift from interpretation to operation makes possible a foundation where rights are upheld by system design rather than interpretive discretion.

This Essay has explored that shifting terrain. It has not proposed regulations or enforcement mechanisms. It has described a landscape and identified materials that now exist for building within it. The statutes do not need amending. They need infrastructure that ensures their words survive the pipeline. The agencies do not need new authority. They need new operational capacity to verify that the law means what it says. The courts do not need new doctrine. They need new forms of proof that allow them to ask what was previously unanswerable: did the standard survive?

The old walls are coming down. The materials for what comes next are already here.

[^1]: See Students for Fair Admissions, Inc. v. President & Fellows of Harvard Coll., 143 S. Ct. 2141 (2023) (restricting race-conscious admissions); Brnovich v. Democratic Nat'l Comm., 141 S. Ct. 2321 (2021) (narrowing Voting Rights Act disparate-impact claims); Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015) (upholding Fair Housing Act disparate impact but imposing limiting standards). See generally HUD's Implementation of the Fair Housing Act's Disparate Impact Standard, 91 Fed. Reg. 1475 (proposed Jan. 14, 2026) (to be codified at 24 C.F.R. pt. 100) (proposing revisions to disparate-impact framework).
[^2]: See, e.g., Solon Barocas & Andrew D. Selbst, Big Data's Disparate Impact, 104 Calif. L. Rev. 671, 677–93 (2016) (analyzing how algorithmic systems can produce discriminatory outcomes even absent discriminatory intent); Joshua A. Kroll et al., Accountable Algorithms, 165 U. Pa. L. Rev. 633, 637–44 (2017) (examining governance challenges posed by opaque automated decisionmaking).
[^3]: See Mobley v. Workday, Inc., No. 23-cv-00770-RFL (N.D. Cal.) (alleging that Workday's AI-based applicant recommendation system violated federal antidiscrimination laws by having a disparate impact on job applicants based on race, age, and disability); Harper v. Sirius XM Radio, LLC, No. 2:25-cv-12007 (E.D. Mich. filed Aug. 4, 2025) (alleging that AI hiring tool resulted in racial discrimination by using educational institutions, employment history, and zip codes as proxies for race); see also EEOC Hearing Explores Potential Benefits and Harms of Artificial Intelligence and Other Automated Systems in Employment Decisions, U.S. EEOC (Jan. 31, 2023), https://perma.cc/5BSK-5BFT (noting concerns that AI hiring tools may perpetuate or amplify bias).
[^4]: This is not to say AI systems cannot discriminate; they can and do. But they discriminate because they are taught to do so—through biased training data, flawed reward modeling, or deployment in contexts that produce discriminatory outcomes. The default, absent such interventions, is neutral processing of signal structure and content. See generally Anya E.R. Prince & Daniel Schwarcz, Proxy Discrimination in the Age of Artificial Intelligence and Big Data, 105 Iowa L. Rev. 1257, 1262–70 (2020) (analyzing how facially neutral algorithmic proxies produce discriminatory effects).
[^5]: See Danielle Keats Citron, Technological Due Process, 85 Wash. U. L. Rev. 1249, 1252–58 (2008) (arguing that automated systems raise distinct procedural concerns because they bypass human judgment and contextual reasoning).
[^6]: See, e.g., Pauline T. Kim, Race-Aware Algorithms: Fairness, Nondiscrimination, and Affirmative Action, 110 Calif. L. Rev. 1539, 1545–52 (2022) (discussing employer use of algorithmic screening tools and their disparate impact potential); Manish Raghavan et al., Mitigating Bias in Algorithmic Hiring: Evaluating Claims and Practices, 2020 ACM Conf. on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency 469, 471–73 (2020) (surveying algorithmic hiring practices and bias mitigation techniques).
[^7]: See Robert Bartlett et al., Consumer-Lending Discrimination in the Tech Era, 143 J. Fin. Econ. 30, 36–40 (2022) (documenting persistent discrimination in algorithmic lending).
[^8]: See Jill Barshay & Sasha Aslanian, Colleges Are Using Big Data to Track Students in an Effort to Boost Graduation Rates, but It Comes at a Cost, Hechinger Rep. (Aug. 6, 2019), https://perma.cc/5CR6-6GHC (describing predictive analytics in higher education admissions and retention).
[^9]: See generally 42 U.S.C. § 2000e-2(a)(1) (prohibiting employment discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin).
[^10]: See 15 U.S.C. § 1691(a)(1) (prohibiting discrimination in credit transactions on the basis of race, color, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, age, or receipt of public assistance).
[^11]: See U.S. Dep't of Educ. Off. of Educ. Tech., Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Teaching and Learning: Insights and Recommendations 5 (2023), https://perma.cc/5XJE-2EFB (recognizing the "accelerating pace of incorporation of AI into mainstream technologies" in the context of education).
[^12]: 42 U.S.C. § 2000e-2(a)(1).
[^13]: 42 U.S.C. § 3604(a), (f).
[^14]: 42 U.S.C. § 12112(b)(5)(A).
[^15]: 15 U.S.C. § 1691(a)(1).
[^16]: See Citron, supra note 5, at 1252–58.
[^17]: See Fed. R. Evid. 901(a) (requiring authentication of evidence by "evidence sufficient to support a finding that the item is what the proponent claims it is"); see also Rebecca Wexler, Life, Liberty, and Trade Secrets: Intellectual Property in the Criminal Justice System, 70 Stan. L. Rev. 1343, 1350–56 (2018) (discussing tension between evidentiary transparency and proprietary interests in algorithmic systems).
[^18]: See, e.g., Cary Coglianese & David Lehr, Regulating by Robot: Administrative Decision Making in the Machine-Learning Era, 105 Geo. L.J. 1147, 1167–72 (2017) (questioning whether existing administrative law frameworks are adequate for AI systems); Kim, supra note 6, at 1585–92 (considering statutory interpretation issues for algorithmic hiring).
[^19]: 42 U.S.C. § 2000e-2(k)(1)(A)(i); see Griggs v. Duke Power Co., 401 U.S. 424, 431 (1971) (establishing business necessity defense).
[^20]: See Chase Mays, Protecting Protected Characteristics: Statutory Solutions for Employment Discrimination Post-Bostock, 77 Vand. L. Rev. 1303, 1310–15 (2024) (discussing how proxies for protected characteristics undermine Title VII's goals).
[^21]: See Vill. of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252, 265–68 (1977) (articulating factors for inferring discriminatory intent from circumstantial evidence); Ricci v. DeStefano, 557 U.S. 557, 577–79 (2009) (discussing tension between disparate treatment and disparate impact theories).
[^22]: See [Author Name Redacted], A Conservation Law for Commitment in Language Under Transformative Compression and Recursive Application (Mar. 19, 2026) (preprint, Zenodo DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18792459) [hereinafter Conservation Law Preprint] (describing operational governance protocol and fidelity verification mechanisms).
[^23]: See Prince & Schwarcz, supra note 4, at 1262–70; Barocas & Selbst, supra note 2, at 694–714.
[^24]: See Algorithmic Proxy Discrimination and Its Regulations, 54 Comput. L. & Sec. Rev. 106021 (2024) (classifying algorithmic proxy discrimination and proposing regulatory frameworks).
[^25]: See Inclusive Cmtys., 576 U.S. at 533–40 (requiring robust causation showing for disparate-impact claims); Brnovich, 141 S. Ct. at 2338–40 (emphasizing state interests in election administration over disparate-impact concerns).
[^26]: See Students for Fair Admissions, 143 S. Ct. at 2161–62 (embracing colorblind interpretation of Equal Protection Clause).
[^27]: See U.S. Dep't of Just. Off. of Legal Pol'y, Artificial Intelligence and Criminal Justice: Final Report 9–11 (2024), https://perma.cc/UZ63-DWAH (discussing agency use of AI in enforcement contexts); see also Kenya Hunter, The Trump Administration Withdrew 11 Pieces of ADA Guidance. How Will It Affect Compliance?, Associated Press (Apr. 8, 2025), https://perma.cc/F46P-MUZ2 (noting shifting agency guidance on disability rights enforcement); White House Off. of Sci. & Tech. Pol'y, Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights: Making Automated Systems Work for the American People (2022), https://perma.cc/8G5L-9X2N (outlining non-binding principles to guide design and deployment of automated systems, including freedom from algorithmic discrimination).
[^28]: Conservation Law Preprint, supra note 22, at § 3.1.
[^29]: See id. at § 7 (documenting failure modes including obligation escalation, scope widening, exception dropping, and modal frame inversion).
[^30]: [Author Name Redacted], Experimental Record for A Conservation Law for Commitment in Language Under Transformative Compression and Recursive Application (EXP-001 to EXP-007), Zenodo DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19105225 [hereinafter Experimental Record]. The 3,950 runs comprise: EXP-001 (30), EXP-002 (1,200), EXP-003 (1,200), EXP-004 (420), EXP-005 (500), EXP-006 (240), and EXP-007 (360).
[^31]: See generally Kroll et al., supra note 2, at 637–44 (examining technical mechanisms for algorithmic accountability without requiring disclosure of proprietary models).
